result.summary: Start with the Defender Secure Score recommendation flyout. It contains the tenant-specific affected objects and Microsoft implementation text. Do not apply broad changes to all users/devices until a pilot succeeds.

steps: Before creating a new policy, check whether Defender already created an Intune security task for this exact recommendation.
In the Intune admin center, go to Endpoint security > Security tasks.
Open the task that matches "Block use of copied or impersonated system tools" if it exists, select Accept, and use the Defender-provided remediation text as the source of truth.
In the Intune admin center, go to Endpoint security > Attack surface reduction.
Select Create Policy.
Platform: Windows. Profile: Attack surface reduction rules. Click Create.
Name the policy "Secure Score ASR - Block use of copied or impersonated system tools". Click Next.
In Configuration settings, expand Attack Surface Reduction.
Set "Block use of copied or impersonated system tools" to Block. For a safer pilot, set it to Audit first, review events, then switch to Block.
Add per-rule exclusions only if a documented business app is blocked in testing. Keep exclusions narrow.
Assign to a pilot device group, then Review + create.
Open Group Policy Management Console (gpmc.msc).
Create or edit the approved ASR GPO.
Go to Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Microsoft Defender Antivirus > Microsoft Defender Exploit Guard > Attack Surface Reduction.
Open Configure Attack Surface Reduction rules.
Set the policy to Enabled.
Click Show.
Add Value name = c0033c00-d16d-4114-a5a0-dc9b3a7d2ceb. Add Value = 1. Value 1 means Block.
Click OK, OK again, then link/security-filter the GPO to your pilot devices.
gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Microsoft Defender Antivirus > Microsoft Defender Exploit Guard > Attack Surface Reduction
gpo.setting_name: Configure Attack Surface Reduction rules

steps: Open the Microsoft Defender portal at security.microsoft.com.
In the left navigation, open Exposure Management or Secure Score, then open Microsoft Secure Score.
Open Recommended actions and search for this exact title: "Block use of copied or impersonated system tools".
Click the recommendation row. Read At a glance, User impact, affected users/devices/entities, and the Implementation section before making any change.
If a Manage button is shown, click Manage to jump to the Microsoft configuration page for this recommendation.

If this is a Device recommendation, open the linked Microsoft Defender Vulnerability Management security recommendation. Device Secure Score actions are remediated from the linked recommendation rather than by manually setting Secure Score status.
Add a note in the recommendation with your change ticket number and planned rollout ring.
After implementation, return to the recommendation and check status. Secure Score points generally take time to refresh; do not mark a device recommendation as complete manually.
defender.policy_name: Block use of copied or impersonated system tools

settings.name: Block use of copied or impersonated system tools

defender.notes: Use this for every recommendation to locate Microsoft's tenant-specific implementation text, affected objects, points, and validation status.

validation_steps: Check Intune per-setting status for pilot devices.
In Defender advanced hunting, look for ASR audit/block events for the pilot group where applicable.
Run Get-MpPreference on a pilot device and verify the rule GUID exists with action Enabled/Block.

rollback.intune: Change the ASR rule to Audit or Not configured in the Intune policy, or remove the pilot assignment.
rollback.gpo: Remove the GUID from Configure Attack Surface Reduction rules, set the value to 2 for Audit, or unlink the GPO from the pilot OU.
rollback.powershell:
# Rollback
Change the ASR rule to Audit or Not configured in the Intune policy, or remove the pilot assignment.

risks: Start with the Defender Secure Score recommendation flyout. It contains the tenant-specific affected objects and Microsoft implementation text. Do not apply broad changes to all users/devices until a pilot succeeds.
Verify compatibility with existing policies before applying.

references.title: Secure Score: Block use of copied or impersonated system tools

scripts.detection:
# Detection — exits 0 if compliant, 1 if not

# Detection example
$pref = Get-MpPreference
$ids = @($pref.AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids)
$actions = @($pref.AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions)
$index = [array]::IndexOf($ids, 'c0033c00-d16d-4114-a5a0-dc9b3a7d2ceb')
if ($index -ge 0 -and $actions[$index] -eq 'Enabled') { exit 0 } else { exit 1 }

# Remediation example
Add-MpPreference -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids c0033c00-d16d-4114-a5a0-dc9b3a7d2ceb -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions Enabled

# Manual verification
Get-MpPreference | Select-Object AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids, AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions
scripts.implementation: # Use the Intune steps above to implement this recommendation
scripts.validation:
# Detection script exits 0 on compliant devices.
# Intune Remediations report shows remediated/succeeded.
# Secure Score/Defender recommendation updates after device telemetry refresh.

rollback.intune: Change the ASR rule to Audit or Not configured in the Intune policy, or remove the pilot assignment.
rollback.gpo: Remove the GUID from Configure Attack Surface Reduction rules, set the value to 2 for Audit, or unlink the GPO from the pilot OU.
rollback.powershell:
# Rollback
Remove or disable the remediation assignment. To remove this specific local rule entry, use policy management or Set-MpPreference according to your standard Defender policy model; avoid leaving local script state fighting Intune/GPO.

steps: Open the Secure Score recommendation "Identify Entra ID privileged accounts that are also privileged in Active Directory" and check whether Microsoft lists a Group Policy path or Windows security baseline mapping.
If Microsoft lists a GPO path, open Group Policy Management Console and create a new pilot GPO instead of editing a broad production GPO immediately.
Configure only the exact setting and value Microsoft lists. Do not choose a similar-looking ADMX setting unless it is explicitly named.
Link the GPO to a pilot OU or apply security filtering to a pilot group.
Run gpupdate /force on one pilot device and confirm the effective setting with gpresult.
Expand the GPO only after the pilot device and Secure Score/Defender telemetry validate successfully.
gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings
gpo.setting_name: Identify Entra ID privileged accounts that are also privileged in Active Directory

steps: Open the Secure Score recommendation "Identify Entra ID privileged accounts that are also privileged in Active Directory" and confirm the recommendation actually has an Entra identity, role, Conditional Access, consent, or authentication component.
Use a least-privileged administrator role for the change. Avoid using Global Administrator unless the task requires it.
Identify the exact users, groups, roles, apps, or policies affected before making changes.
For Conditional Access, create or modify the policy in Report-only mode first and exclude approved emergency access accounts.
Review Entra sign-in logs or audit logs for pilot impact.
Turn the policy On or save the production setting only after pilot validation.
entra.notes: Conditional: applicable only when the finding involves Entra identities, hybrid privileged accounts, leaked credentials, or cloud roles.

defender.portal_path: Microsoft Defender portal > Secure Score or Defender for Identity security posture assessments
steps: Open Microsoft Defender portal > Secure Score or Defender for Identity security posture assessments.
Search/open the exact assessment: Identify Entra ID privileged accounts that are also privileged in Active Directory.
Open the affected entities list. Export or copy affected users, groups, computers, GPOs, certificate templates, servers, or domain controllers.
Read the Microsoft remediation text for that assessment before changing AD/AD CS. These recommendations are entity-specific.
Make the AD/Entra/identity change only to the affected entities named by the assessment.
For group membership findings, remove unnecessary privileged membership after owner approval.
For account attribute findings, correct the exact insecure attribute shown by Defender.
For stale/dormant accounts, disable/remove only after owner validation.
defender.notes: Use Defender for Identity assessment output. These are usually AD/AD CS/entity remediation tasks, not generic Intune policies.

steps: Before creating a new policy, check whether Defender already created an Intune security task for this exact recommendation.
In the Intune admin center, go to Endpoint security > Security tasks.
Open the task that matches "Require LDAP server signing to ensure integrity of directory traffic" if it exists, select Accept, and use the Defender-provided remediation text as the source of truth.
In the Intune admin center, go to Devices > Manage devices > Configuration > Create > New policy.
Select Platform = Windows 10 and later unless the recommendation is explicitly for another OS. Select Profile type = Settings catalog. Click Create.
On Basics, name the policy "Secure Score - 3 - Require LDAP server signing to ensure integrity of directory traffic". Click Next.
On Configuration settings, click Add settings. In Search, search for: Require LDAP server signing to ensure integrity of directory traffic.
Add the matching Microsoft setting and configure it to: ensure integrity of directory traffic. Do not pick a look-alike setting unless the Secure Score Implementation section confirms it.
Click Next. Assign first to a pilot device group. Do not assign to All devices for the first test unless your change process allows it.
Click Next through Scope tags and Applicability rules if not used. On Review + create, click Create.
Monitor deployment under Devices > Configuration > the policy > Device and user check-in status and Per-setting status.
Open Group Policy Management Console (gpmc.msc).
Create a new GPO for this recommendation or edit your approved security baseline GPO.
Go to: Domain Controllers GPO > Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options.
Open the setting: Domain controller: LDAP server signing requirements.
Set it to: Require signing.
Link the GPO to a pilot OU or apply security filtering to a pilot group first.
On a pilot device, run gpupdate /force or wait for normal Group Policy refresh.

steps: Open https://intune.microsoft.com.

Open Group Policy Management Console: gpmc.msc.
Create a new pilot GPO or edit your approved security baseline GPO.

Open the policy setting: PasswordManagerEnabled.
Set it to: Disabled / false.
Click OK/Apply.
Link the GPO only to a pilot OU first.
On a pilot device, run gpupdate /force or wait for normal policy refresh.
Reboot the pilot device if the setting requires a restart.
After testing, link the GPO to the wider production OU.

steps: Sign in to the Microsoft Entra admin center with at least Conditional Access Administrator or Security Administrator.
Go to Protection > Conditional Access > Policies.
Select New policy. Name it Secure Score - Admin sign-in frequency and non-persistent browser sessions.
Under Users, include Directory roles and select your administrative roles. Exclude at least one break-glass emergency access account if your organisation uses one.
Under Target resources, choose All cloud apps unless your change board requires a narrower pilot scope.
Under Conditions, configure any pilot conditions you need before broad rollout.
Under Session, select Sign-in frequency and configure the interval required by your Secure Score recommendation. Also select Persistent browser session and set it to Never persistent.
Set Enable policy to Report-only for pilot testing first, review sign-in logs, then change to On when approved.

result.summary: Start with the Defender Secure Score recommendation flyout. It contains the tenant-specific affected objects and Microsoft implementation text. Do not apply broad changes to all users/devices until a pilot succeeds.

steps: Open the Secure Score recommendation "Change password for krbtgt account" and check whether Microsoft lists a Group Policy path or Windows security baseline mapping.
If Microsoft lists a GPO path, open Group Policy Management Console and create a new pilot GPO instead of editing a broad production GPO immediately.
Configure only the exact setting and value Microsoft lists. Do not choose a similar-looking ADMX setting unless it is explicitly named.
Link the GPO to a pilot OU or apply security filtering to a pilot group.
Run gpupdate /force on one pilot device and confirm the effective setting with gpresult.
Expand the GPO only after the pilot device and Secure Score/Defender telemetry validate successfully.
gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings
gpo.setting_name: Change password for krbtgt account

steps: Open the Secure Score recommendation "Change password for krbtgt account" and confirm the recommendation actually has an Entra identity, role, Conditional Access, consent, or authentication component.
Use a least-privileged administrator role for the change. Avoid using Global Administrator unless the task requires it.
Identify the exact users, groups, roles, apps, or policies affected before making changes.
For Conditional Access, create or modify the policy in Report-only mode first and exclude approved emergency access accounts.
Review Entra sign-in logs or audit logs for pilot impact.
Turn the policy On or save the production setting only after pilot validation.
entra.notes: Conditional: applicable only when the finding involves Entra identities, hybrid privileged accounts, leaked credentials, or cloud roles.

defender.portal_path: Microsoft Defender portal > Secure Score or Defender for Identity security posture assessments
steps: Open Microsoft Defender portal > Secure Score or Defender for Identity security posture assessments.
Search/open the exact assessment: Change password for krbtgt account.
Open the affected entities list. Export or copy affected users, groups, computers, GPOs, certificate templates, servers, or domain controllers.
Read the Microsoft remediation text for that assessment before changing AD/AD CS. These recommendations are entity-specific.
Make the AD/Entra/identity change only to the affected entities named by the assessment.
For group membership findings, remove unnecessary privileged membership after owner approval.
For account attribute findings, correct the exact insecure attribute shown by Defender.
For stale/dormant accounts, disable/remove only after owner validation.
defender.notes: Use Defender for Identity assessment output. These are usually AD/AD CS/entity remediation tasks, not generic Intune policies.

validation_steps: gpresult shows the pilot GPO is applied.
The local effective setting matches the target value.
Secure Score/Defender status improves after telemetry refresh.

rollback.gpo: Set the policy to Not Configured in Group Policy Management
rollback.defender: Use your AD/AD CS backup/change process to reverse only the changed object/permission/policy.

gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings
gpo.setting_name: Ensure user consent to apps accessing company data on their behalf is not allowed

steps: Sign in to the Microsoft Entra admin center.
Go to Identity > Applications > Enterprise applications > Consent and permissions > User consent settings.
Set user consent so users are not allowed to consent to applications, or use the Microsoft-recommended verified publisher/low-impact option if your tenant policy requires that instead.
Go to Admin consent settings and enable the admin consent workflow so users can request approval instead of granting consent themselves.
Review + save the settings.
entra.notes: Use Microsoft Entra admin center / Conditional Access / Identity Protection.

steps: Open the Microsoft Defender portal at security.microsoft.com.
In the left navigation, open Exposure Management or Secure Score, then open Microsoft Secure Score.
Open Recommended actions and search for this exact title: "Ensure user consent to apps accessing company data on their behalf is not allowed".
Click the recommendation row. Read At a glance, User impact, affected users/devices/entities, and the Implementation section before making any change.
If a Manage button is shown, click Manage to jump to the Microsoft configuration page for this recommendation.

If this is a Device recommendation, open the linked Microsoft Defender Vulnerability Management security recommendation. Device Secure Score actions are remediated from the linked recommendation rather than by manually setting Secure Score status.
Add a note in the recommendation with your change ticket number and planned rollout ring.
After implementation, return to the recommendation and check status. Secure Score points generally take time to refresh; do not mark a device recommendation as complete manually.
defender.notes: Use this for every recommendation to locate Microsoft's tenant-specific implementation text, affected objects, points, and validation status.

validation_steps: Confirm the Secure Score recommendation no longer shows exposed users/devices/entities or that Microsoft shows the action as completed/points awarded.
For device recommendations, verify affected device count in Defender Vulnerability Management after telemetry refresh.

rollback.gpo: Set the policy to Not Configured in Group Policy Management
rollback.entra: Set the policy back to Report-only or Off, or revert the setting to its previous value, only through approved change control.

steps: Before creating a new policy, check whether Defender already created an Intune security task for this exact recommendation.
In the Intune admin center, go to Endpoint security > Security tasks.
Open the task that matches "Encrypt LDAP client traffic to protect sensitive data in transit" if it exists, select Accept, and use the Defender-provided remediation text as the source of truth.
In the Intune admin center, go to Devices > Manage devices > Configuration > Create > New policy.
Select Platform = Windows 10 and later unless the recommendation is explicitly for another OS. Select Profile type = Settings catalog. Click Create.
On Basics, name the policy "Secure Score - 20 - Encrypt LDAP client traffic to protect sensitive data in transit". Click Next.
On Configuration settings, click Add settings. In Search, search for: Encrypt LDAP client traffic to protect sensitive data in transit.
Add the matching Microsoft setting and configure it to: protect sensitive data in transit. Do not pick a look-alike setting unless the Secure Score Implementation section confirms it.
Click Next. Assign first to a pilot device group. Do not assign to All devices for the first test unless your change process allows it.
Click Next through Scope tags and Applicability rules if not used. On Review + create, click Create.
Monitor deployment under Devices > Configuration > the policy > Device and user check-in status and Per-setting status.
Open the Secure Score recommendation and check whether Microsoft lists a GPO path or Windows security baseline setting.
If Microsoft lists a GPO path, create/edit a pilot GPO and configure only that exact setting/value.
If no GPO path is listed, do not guess. Use Intune Settings Catalog, Defender security task, or the product-specific method instead.

gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings
gpo.setting_name: Ensure users installing Outlook add-ins is not allowed

steps: Open the Secure Score recommendation "Ensure users installing Outlook add-ins is not allowed" and confirm the recommendation actually has an Entra identity, role, Conditional Access, consent, or authentication component.
Use a least-privileged administrator role for the change. Avoid using Global Administrator unless the task requires it.
Identify the exact users, groups, roles, apps, or policies affected before making changes.
For Conditional Access, create or modify the policy in Report-only mode first and exclude approved emergency access accounts.
Review Entra sign-in logs or audit logs for pilot impact.
Turn the policy On or save the production setting only after pilot validation.
entra.notes: Conditional: Entra is relevant for admin roles/groups used to scope or administer the policy, but not the primary implementation plane.

steps: Open the Microsoft Defender portal at security.microsoft.com.
In the left navigation, open Exposure Management or Secure Score, then open Microsoft Secure Score.
Open Recommended actions and search for this exact title: "Ensure users installing Outlook add-ins is not allowed".
Click the recommendation row. Read At a glance, User impact, affected users/devices/entities, and the Implementation section before making any change.
If a Manage button is shown, click Manage to jump to the Microsoft configuration page for this recommendation.

If this is a Device recommendation, open the linked Microsoft Defender Vulnerability Management security recommendation. Device Secure Score actions are remediated from the linked recommendation rather than by manually setting Secure Score status.
Add a note in the recommendation with your change ticket number and planned rollout ring.
After implementation, return to the recommendation and check status. Secure Score points generally take time to refresh; do not mark a device recommendation as complete manually.
defender.notes: Use this for every recommendation to locate Microsoft's tenant-specific implementation text, affected objects, points, and validation status.

validation_steps: Confirm the Secure Score recommendation no longer shows exposed users/devices/entities or that Microsoft shows the action as completed/points awarded.
For device recommendations, verify affected device count in Defender Vulnerability Management after telemetry refresh.

gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings
gpo.setting_name: Ensure multifactor authentication is enabled for all users in administrative roles

steps: Sign in to the Microsoft Entra admin center.
Go to Protection > Conditional Access > Policies > New policy.
Name the policy Secure Score - Require MFA for admin roles.
Under Users, include Directory roles and select all administrative roles that are in use. Exclude only approved emergency access accounts.
Under Target resources, choose All cloud apps.
Under Grant, select Grant access and require multifactor authentication, or require an authentication strength if your organisation uses authentication strengths.
Set the policy to Report-only first, confirm no lockout risk, then set it to On.

steps: Before creating a new policy, check whether Defender already created an Intune security task for this exact recommendation.
In the Intune admin center, go to Endpoint security > Security tasks.

Open the task that matches "Turn on Microsoft Defender Antivirus PUA protection in block mode for Linux" if it exists, select Accept, and use the Defender-provided remediation text as the source of truth.
In the Intune admin center, go to Devices > Manage devices > Configuration > Create > New policy.
Select Platform = Windows 10 and later unless the recommendation is explicitly for another OS. Select Profile type = Settings catalog. Click Create.
On Basics, name the policy "Secure Score - 29 - Turn on Microsoft Defender Antivirus PUA protection in block mode for Linux". Click Next.
On Configuration settings, click Add settings. In Search, search for: Turn on Microsoft Defender Antivirus PUA protection in block mode for Linux.

Add the matching Microsoft setting and configure it to: Enabled / enforced as stated in the recommendation. Do not pick a look-alike setting unless the Secure Score Implementation section confirms it.
Click Next. Assign first to a pilot device group. Do not assign to All devices for the first test unless your change process allows it.
Click Next through Scope tags and Applicability rules if not used. On Review + create, click Create.
Monitor deployment under Devices > Configuration > the policy > Device and user check-in status and Per-setting status.
Open the Secure Score recommendation and check whether Microsoft lists a GPO path or Windows security baseline setting.
If Microsoft lists a GPO path, create/edit a pilot GPO and configure only that exact setting/value.
If no GPO path is listed, do not guess. Use Intune Settings Catalog, Defender security task, or the product-specific method instead.

steps: Before creating a new policy, check whether Defender already created an Intune security task for this exact recommendation.
In the Intune admin center, go to Endpoint security > Security tasks.
Open the task that matches "Enable EDR in block mode" if it exists, select Accept, and use the Defender-provided remediation text as the source of truth.

steps: Open Group Policy Management Console.
Edit the target GPO.
Go to Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Microsoft Defender Antivirus > Features.
Open Enable EDR in block mode.
Set it to Enabled and select OK.
Pilot the GPO before broad rollout.

steps: Open https://intune.microsoft.com.
Go to Devices > Configuration.
Select Create > New policy.
Platform: Windows 10 and later.
Profile type: Settings catalog.
Select Create.
Name the profile: Secure Score - Enable 'Require domain users to elevate when setting a network's location'.
Select Add settings.
In the Settings picker search box, search for: Require domain users to elevate when setting a network's location OR Network Connections.
Select the exact setting that matches the name in settings_to_configure. Do not select a similarly named setting from another product area.
Configure the setting value to: Enabled.
Select Next until Assignments.
Assign only to your pilot device group first.
Select Review + create > Create.
After pilot success, expand the assignment to the production group.
Open Group Policy Management Console: gpmc.msc.
Create a new pilot GPO or edit your approved security baseline GPO.
Go to: Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Network > Network Connections.
Open the policy setting: Require domain users to elevate when setting a network's location.
Set it to: Enabled.
Click OK/Apply.

After testing, link the GPO to the wider production OU.

steps: Before creating a new policy, check whether Defender already created an Intune security task for this exact recommendation.
In the Intune admin center, go to Endpoint security > Security tasks.
Open the task that matches "Block process creations originating from PSExec and WMI commands" if it exists, select Accept, and use the Defender-provided remediation text as the source of truth.
In the Intune admin center, go to Endpoint security > Attack surface reduction.
Select Create Policy.
Platform: Windows. Profile: Attack surface reduction rules. Click Create.
Name the policy "Secure Score ASR - Block process creations originating from PSExec and WMI commands". Click Next.
In Configuration settings, expand Attack Surface Reduction.
Set "Process creations originating from PSExec and WMI commands" to Block. For a safer pilot, set it to Audit first, review events, then switch to Block.
Add per-rule exclusions only if a documented business app is blocked in testing. Keep exclusions narrow.
Assign to a pilot device group, then Review + create.
Open Group Policy Management Console (gpmc.msc).
Create or edit the approved ASR GPO.
Go to Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Microsoft Defender Antivirus > Microsoft Defender Exploit Guard > Attack Surface Reduction.
Open Configure Attack Surface Reduction rules.
Set the policy to Enabled.
Click Show.
Add Value name = d1e49aac-8f56-4280-b9ba-993a6d77406c. Add Value = 1. Value 1 means Block.
Click OK, OK again, then link/security-filter the GPO to your pilot devices.

scripts.detection:
# Detection — exits 0 if compliant, 1 if not

# Detection example
$pref = Get-MpPreference
$ids = @($pref.AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids)
$actions = @($pref.AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions)
$index = [array]::IndexOf($ids, 'd1e49aac-8f56-4280-b9ba-993a6d77406c')
if ($index -ge 0 -and $actions[$index] -eq 'Enabled') { exit 0 } else { exit 1 }

# Remediation example
Add-MpPreference -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids d1e49aac-8f56-4280-b9ba-993a6d77406c -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions Enabled

# Manual verification
Get-MpPreference | Select-Object AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids, AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions
scripts.implementation: # Use the Intune steps above to implement this recommendation
scripts.validation:
# Detection script exits 0 on compliant devices.
# Intune Remediations report shows remediated/succeeded.
# Secure Score/Defender recommendation updates after device telemetry refresh.

rollback.gpo: Remove the GUID from Configure Attack Surface Reduction rules, set the value to 2 for Audit, or unlink the GPO from the pilot OU.
rollback.powershell:
# Rollback
Remove or disable the remediation assignment. To remove this specific local rule entry, use policy management or Set-MpPreference according to your standard Defender policy model; avoid leaving local script state fighting Intune/GPO.

steps: Open https://intune.microsoft.com.
Go to Endpoint security > Firewall.
Create or edit a Microsoft Defender Firewall Rules policy.
Add a rule: Direction = Outbound, Action = Block, Program path = %SystemRoot%\System32\mshta.exe.
Add a second rule for 64-bit devices: Direction = Outbound, Action = Block, Program path = %SystemRoot%\SysWOW64\mshta.exe.
Assign to a pilot device group.
Verify no business app requires mshta outbound traffic, then expand deployment.
Open Group Policy Management Console.
Create or edit a pilot GPO.
Go to Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security > Outbound Rules.
Right-click Outbound Rules > New Rule.
Rule Type: Program.
Program path: %SystemRoot%\System32\mshta.exe.
Action: Block the connection.
Profiles: select Domain, Private, and Public unless your change process scopes differently.
Name: Secure Score - Block mshta outbound.
Repeat the same process for %SystemRoot%\SysWOW64\mshta.exe on 64-bit systems.
Link to pilot OU and test.
gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security > Outbound Rules

scripts.detection:
# Detection — exits 0 if compliant, 1 if not

# Create outbound block rules for mshta.exe
New-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "Secure Score - Block mshta outbound (System32)" -Direction Outbound -Program "$env:WINDIR\System32\mshta.exe" -Action Block -Profile Any
if (Test-Path "$env:WINDIR\SysWOW64\mshta.exe") { New-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "Secure Score - Block mshta outbound (SysWOW64)" -Direction Outbound -Program "$env:WINDIR\SysWOW64\mshta.exe" -Action Block -Profile Any }
scripts.implementation: # Use the Intune steps above to implement this recommendation
scripts.validation: # Run PowerShell: Get-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "Secure Score - Block mshta outbound*" | Select-Object DisplayName,Enabled,Direction,Action

steps: Before creating a new policy, check whether Defender already created an Intune security task for this exact recommendation.

Create a new GPO for this recommendation or edit your approved security baseline GPO.

Open the setting: Accounts: Administrator account status.

Link the GPO to a pilot OU or apply security filtering to a pilot group first.

scripts.detection:
# Detection — exits 0 if compliant, 1 if not

# Detection example
$pref = Get-MpPreference
$ids = @($pref.AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids)
$actions = @($pref.AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions)
$index = [array]::IndexOf($ids, '33ddedf1-c6e0-47cb-833e-de6133960387')
if ($index -ge 0 -and $actions[$index] -eq 'Enabled') { exit 0 } else { exit 1 }

# Remediation example
Add-MpPreference -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids 33ddedf1-c6e0-47cb-833e-de6133960387 -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions Enabled

# Manual verification
Get-MpPreference | Select-Object AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids, AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions
scripts.implementation: # Use the Intune steps above to implement this recommendation
scripts.validation:
# Detection script exits 0 on compliant devices.
# Intune Remediations report shows remediated/succeeded.
# Secure Score/Defender recommendation updates after device telemetry refresh.

rollback.intune: Change the ASR rule to Audit or Not configured in the Intune policy, or remove the pilot assignment.
rollback.gpo: Remove the GUID from Configure Attack Surface Reduction rules, set the value to 2 for Audit, or unlink the GPO from the pilot OU.
rollback.powershell:
# Rollback
Remove or disable the remediation assignment. To remove this specific local rule entry, use policy management or Set-MpPreference according to your standard Defender policy model; avoid leaving local script state fighting Intune/GPO.

result.summary: Start with the Defender Secure Score recommendation flyout. It contains the tenant-specific affected objects and Microsoft implementation text. Do not apply broad changes to all users/devices until a pilot succeeds.

steps: Before creating a new policy, check whether Defender already created an Intune security task for this exact recommendation.
In the Intune admin center, go to Endpoint security > Security tasks.
Open the task that matches "Fix unquoted service path for Windows services" if it exists, select Accept, and use the Defender-provided remediation text as the source of truth.
In the Intune admin center, go to Devices > Manage devices > Configuration > Create > New policy.
Select Platform = Windows 10 and later unless the recommendation is explicitly for another OS. Select Profile type = Settings catalog. Click Create.
On Basics, name the policy "Secure Score - 64 - Fix unquoted service path for Windows services". Click Next.
On Configuration settings, click Add settings. In Search, search for: Fix unquoted service path for Windows services.

Add the matching Microsoft setting and configure it to: Apply target state from recommendation title: Fix unquoted service path for Windows services. Do not pick a look-alike setting unless the Secure Score Implementation section confirms it.
Click Next. Assign first to a pilot device group. Do not assign to All devices for the first test unless your change process allows it.
Click Next through Scope tags and Applicability rules if not used. On Review + create, click Create.
Monitor deployment under Devices > Configuration > the policy > Device and user check-in status and Per-setting status.
Open the Secure Score recommendation and check whether Microsoft lists a GPO path or Windows security baseline setting.
If Microsoft lists a GPO path, create/edit a pilot GPO and configure only that exact setting/value.
If no GPO path is listed, do not guess. Use Intune Settings Catalog, Defender security task, or the product-specific method instead.
gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings
gpo.setting_name: Fix unquoted service path for Windows services

steps: Open the Microsoft Defender portal at security.microsoft.com.
In the left navigation, open Exposure Management or Secure Score, then open Microsoft Secure Score.
Open Recommended actions and search for this exact title: "Fix unquoted service path for Windows services".
Click the recommendation row. Read At a glance, User impact, affected users/devices/entities, and the Implementation section before making any change.
If a Manage button is shown, click Manage to jump to the Microsoft configuration page for this recommendation.

If this is a Device recommendation, open the linked Microsoft Defender Vulnerability Management security recommendation. Device Secure Score actions are remediated from the linked recommendation rather than by manually setting Secure Score status.
Add a note in the recommendation with your change ticket number and planned rollout ring.
After implementation, return to the recommendation and check status. Secure Score points generally take time to refresh; do not mark a device recommendation as complete manually.

Return to Defender/Secure Score and verify the recommendation status after refresh.

steps: Before creating a new policy, check whether Defender already created an Intune security task for this exact recommendation.
In the Intune admin center, go to Endpoint security > Security tasks.
Open the task that matches "Block Office communication application from creating child processes" if it exists, select Accept, and use the Defender-provided remediation text as the source of truth.
In the Intune admin center, go to Endpoint security > Attack surface reduction.
Select Create Policy.
Platform: Windows. Profile: Attack surface reduction rules. Click Create.
Name the policy "Secure Score ASR - Block Office communication application from creating child processes". Click Next.
In Configuration settings, expand Attack Surface Reduction.
Set "Process creation from Office communication products (beta)" to Block. For a safer pilot, set it to Audit first, review events, then switch to Block.
Add per-rule exclusions only if a documented business app is blocked in testing. Keep exclusions narrow.
Assign to a pilot device group, then Review + create.
Open Group Policy Management Console (gpmc.msc).
Create or edit the approved ASR GPO.
Go to Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Microsoft Defender Antivirus > Microsoft Defender Exploit Guard > Attack Surface Reduction.
Open Configure Attack Surface Reduction rules.
Set the policy to Enabled.
Click Show.
Add Value name = 26190899-1602-49e8-8b27-eb1d0a1ce869. Add Value = 1. Value 1 means Block.
Click OK, OK again, then link/security-filter the GPO to your pilot devices.

scripts.detection:
# Detection — exits 0 if compliant, 1 if not

# Detection example
$pref = Get-MpPreference
$ids = @($pref.AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids)
$actions = @($pref.AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions)
$index = [array]::IndexOf($ids, '26190899-1602-49e8-8b27-eb1d0a1ce869')
if ($index -ge 0 -and $actions[$index] -eq 'Enabled') { exit 0 } else { exit 1 }

# Remediation example
Add-MpPreference -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids 26190899-1602-49e8-8b27-eb1d0a1ce869 -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions Enabled

# Manual verification
Get-MpPreference | Select-Object AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids, AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions

steps: Before creating a new policy, check whether Defender already created an Intune security task for this exact recommendation.
In the Intune admin center, go to Endpoint security > Security tasks.
Open the task that matches "Block executable content from email client and webmail" if it exists, select Accept, and use the Defender-provided remediation text as the source of truth.
In the Intune admin center, go to Endpoint security > Attack surface reduction.
Select Create Policy.
Platform: Windows. Profile: Attack surface reduction rules. Click Create.
Name the policy "Secure Score ASR - Block executable content from email client and webmail". Click Next.
In Configuration settings, expand Attack Surface Reduction.
Set "Executable content from email client and webmail" to Block. For a safer pilot, set it to Audit first, review events, then switch to Block.
Add per-rule exclusions only if a documented business app is blocked in testing. Keep exclusions narrow.
Assign to a pilot device group, then Review + create.

Create or edit the approved ASR GPO.

Open Configure Attack Surface Reduction rules.
Set the policy to Enabled.
Click Show.
Add Value name = be9ba2d9-53ea-4cdc-84e5-9b1eeee46550. Add Value = 1. Value 1 means Block.
Click OK, OK again, then link/security-filter the GPO to your pilot devices.

Platform: Windows. Profile: Attack surface reduction rules. Click Create.
Name the policy "Secure Score ASR - Block Office applications from injecting code into other processes". Click Next.
In Configuration settings, expand Attack Surface Reduction.
Set "Office apps injecting code into other processes (no exceptions)" to Block. For a safer pilot, set it to Audit first, review events, then switch to Block.
Add per-rule exclusions only if a documented business app is blocked in testing. Keep exclusions narrow.
Assign to a pilot device group, then Review + create.
Open Group Policy Management Console (gpmc.msc).
Create or edit the approved ASR GPO.
Go to Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Microsoft Defender Antivirus > Microsoft Defender Exploit Guard > Attack Surface Reduction.
Open Configure Attack Surface Reduction rules.
Set the policy to Enabled.
Click Show.
Add Value name = 75668c1f-73b5-4cf0-bb93-3ecf5cb7cc84. Add Value = 1. Value 1 means Block.
Click OK, OK again, then link/security-filter the GPO to your pilot devices.
gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Microsoft Defender Antivirus > Microsoft Defender Exploit Guard > Attack Surface Reduction
gpo.setting_name: Configure Attack Surface Reduction rules

scripts.detection:
# Detection — exits 0 if compliant, 1 if not

# Detection example
$pref = Get-MpPreference
$ids = @($pref.AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids)
$actions = @($pref.AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions)
$index = [array]::IndexOf($ids, '75668c1f-73b5-4cf0-bb93-3ecf5cb7cc84')
if ($index -ge 0 -and $actions[$index] -eq 'Enabled') { exit 0 } else { exit 1 }

# Remediation example
Add-MpPreference -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids 75668c1f-73b5-4cf0-bb93-3ecf5cb7cc84 -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions Enabled

# Manual verification
Get-MpPreference | Select-Object AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids, AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions
scripts.implementation: # Use the Intune steps above to implement this recommendation
scripts.validation:
# Detection script exits 0 on compliant devices.
# Intune Remediations report shows remediated/succeeded.
# Secure Score/Defender recommendation updates after device telemetry refresh.

rollback.intune: Change the ASR rule to Audit or Not configured in the Intune policy, or remove the pilot assignment.
rollback.gpo: Remove the GUID from Configure Attack Surface Reduction rules, set the value to 2 for Audit, or unlink the GPO from the pilot OU.
rollback.powershell:
# Rollback
Remove or disable the remediation assignment. To remove this specific local rule entry, use policy management or Set-MpPreference according to your standard Defender policy model; avoid leaving local script state fighting Intune/GPO.

steps: Before creating a new policy, check whether Defender already created an Intune security task for this exact recommendation.
In the Intune admin center, go to Endpoint security > Security tasks.
Open the task that matches "Disable Anonymous enumeration of shares" if it exists, select Accept, and use the Defender-provided remediation text as the source of truth.
In the Intune admin center, go to Devices > Manage devices > Configuration > Create > New policy.
Select Platform = Windows 10 and later unless the recommendation is explicitly for another OS. Select Profile type = Settings catalog. Click Create.
On Basics, name the policy "Secure Score - 84 - Disable Anonymous enumeration of shares". Click Next.
On Configuration settings, click Add settings. In Search, search for: Disable Anonymous enumeration of shares.
Add the matching Microsoft setting and configure it to: Disabled as stated in the recommendation. Do not pick a look-alike setting unless the Secure Score Implementation section confirms it.
Click Next. Assign first to a pilot device group. Do not assign to All devices for the first test unless your change process allows it.
Click Next through Scope tags and Applicability rules if not used. On Review + create, click Create.
Monitor deployment under Devices > Configuration > the policy > Device and user check-in status and Per-setting status.
Open Group Policy Management Console (gpmc.msc).
Create a new GPO for this recommendation or edit your approved security baseline GPO.
Go to: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options.
Open the setting: Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares.
Set it to: Enabled.
Link the GPO to a pilot OU or apply security filtering to a pilot group first.
On a pilot device, run gpupdate /force or wait for normal Group Policy refresh.

steps: Before creating a new policy, check whether Defender already created an Intune security task for this exact recommendation.
In the Intune admin center, go to Endpoint security > Security tasks.
Open the task that matches "Block Win32 API calls from Office macros" if it exists, select Accept, and use the Defender-provided remediation text as the source of truth.
In the Intune admin center, go to Endpoint security > Attack surface reduction.
Select Create Policy.
Platform: Windows. Profile: Attack surface reduction rules. Click Create.
Name the policy "Secure Score ASR - Block Win32 API calls from Office macros". Click Next.
In Configuration settings, expand Attack Surface Reduction.
Set "Win32 imports from Office macro code" to Block. For a safer pilot, set it to Audit first, review events, then switch to Block.
Add per-rule exclusions only if a documented business app is blocked in testing. Keep exclusions narrow.
Assign to a pilot device group, then Review + create.
Open Group Policy Management Console (gpmc.msc).
Create or edit the approved ASR GPO.
Go to Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Microsoft Defender Antivirus > Microsoft Defender Exploit Guard > Attack Surface Reduction.
Open Configure Attack Surface Reduction rules.
Set the policy to Enabled.
Click Show.
Add Value name = 92e97fa1-2edf-4476-bdd6-9dd0b4dddc7b. Add Value = 1. Value 1 means Block.
Click OK, OK again, then link/security-filter the GPO to your pilot devices.

scripts.detection:
# Detection — exits 0 if compliant, 1 if not

# Detection example
$pref = Get-MpPreference
$ids = @($pref.AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids)
$actions = @($pref.AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions)
$index = [array]::IndexOf($ids, '92e97fa1-2edf-4476-bdd6-9dd0b4dddc7b')
if ($index -ge 0 -and $actions[$index] -eq 'Enabled') { exit 0 } else { exit 1 }

# Remediation example
Add-MpPreference -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids 92e97fa1-2edf-4476-bdd6-9dd0b4dddc7b -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions Enabled

# Manual verification
Get-MpPreference | Select-Object AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids, AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions
scripts.implementation: # Use the Intune steps above to implement this recommendation
scripts.validation:
# Detection script exits 0 on compliant devices.
# Intune Remediations report shows remediated/succeeded.
# Secure Score/Defender recommendation updates after device telemetry refresh.

rollback.intune: Change the ASR rule to Audit or Not configured in the Intune policy, or remove the pilot assignment.
rollback.gpo: Remove the GUID from Configure Attack Surface Reduction rules, set the value to 2 for Audit, or unlink the GPO from the pilot OU.
rollback.powershell:
# Rollback
Remove or disable the remediation assignment. To remove this specific local rule entry, use policy management or Set-MpPreference according to your standard Defender policy model; avoid leaving local script state fighting Intune/GPO.

references.title: Secure Score: Disable 'Continue running background apps when Google Chrome is closed'

scripts.detection:
# Detection — exits 0 if compliant, 1 if not

# Set Chrome policy BackgroundModeEnabled via registry for pilot/remediation
New-Item -Path "HKLM:\Software\Policies\Google\Chrome" -Force | Out-Null
New-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\Software\Policies\Google\Chrome" -Name BackgroundModeEnabled -PropertyType DWord -Value 0 -Force
scripts.implementation: # Use the Intune steps above to implement this recommendation
scripts.validation:
# Run PowerShell: Get-ItemProperty "HKLM:\Software\Policies\Google\Chrome" | Select-Object BackgroundModeEnabled
# Open Chrome and browse to chrome://policy, then reload policies and confirm BackgroundModeEnabled.

rollback.intune: Set BackgroundModeEnabled back to Not configured or previous value and re-check chrome://policy.
rollback.gpo: Unlink the GPO or set the policy back to Not Configured/previous value.; Run gpupdate /force on a pilot device and confirm rollback with gpresult.
rollback.powershell:
# Rollback
Set BackgroundModeEnabled back to Not configured or previous value and re-check chrome://policy.

steps: Open https://intune.microsoft.com.
Go to Endpoint security > Firewall.
Select Create Policy.
Platform: Windows 10 and later.
Profile: Microsoft Defender Firewall.
Name the policy: Secure Score - Turn on Microsoft Defender Firewall.
Open Configuration settings.
Configure these settings: Domain profile Firewall = True; Private profile Firewall = True; Public profile Firewall = True.
Select Next until Assignments.
Assign to a pilot device group first, then Review + create.
After pilot success, assign to production device groups.
intune.value: Enabled for Domain, Private, and Public profiles

steps: Open Group Policy Management Console: gpmc.msc.
Create a new pilot GPO or edit your approved security baseline GPO.
Go to: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security > Windows Defender Firewall Properties.
Open the policy setting: Domain, Private, and Public Profile tabs.
Set it to: Firewall state = On.
Click OK/Apply.
Link the GPO only to a pilot OU first.
On a pilot device, run gpupdate /force or wait for normal policy refresh.
Reboot the pilot device if the setting requires a restart.
After testing, link the GPO to the wider production OU.
gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security > Windows Defender Firewall Properties
gpo.setting_name: Domain, Private, and Public Profile tabs

steps: Open the Microsoft Defender portal at security.microsoft.com.
In the left navigation, open Exposure Management or Secure Score, then open Microsoft Secure Score.
Open Recommended actions and search for this exact title: "Turn on Microsoft Defender Firewall".
Click the recommendation row. Read At a glance, User impact, affected users/devices/entities, and the Implementation section before making any change.
If a Manage button is shown, click Manage to jump to the Microsoft configuration page for this recommendation.

If this is a Device recommendation, open the linked Microsoft Defender Vulnerability Management security recommendation. Device Secure Score actions are remediated from the linked recommendation rather than by manually setting Secure Score status.
Add a note in the recommendation with your change ticket number and planned rollout ring.
After implementation, return to the recommendation and check status. Secure Score points generally take time to refresh; do not mark a device recommendation as complete manually.

Click Next. Assign first to a pilot device group. Do not assign to All devices for the first test unless your change process allows it.
Click Next through Scope tags and Applicability rules if not used. On Review + create, click Create.
Monitor deployment under Devices > Configuration > the policy > Device and user check-in status and Per-setting status.

If Microsoft lists a GPO path, create/edit a pilot GPO and configure only that exact setting/value.
If no GPO path is listed, do not guess. Use Intune Settings Catalog, Defender security task, or the product-specific method instead.

steps: Sign in to the Microsoft Entra admin center.
Go to Protection > Conditional Access > Policies > New policy.
Name the policy Secure Score - Require MFA for all users.
Under Users, include All users. Exclude approved emergency access accounts and service accounts that cannot use interactive MFA.
Under Target resources, choose All cloud apps unless your pilot needs a smaller scope.
Under Grant, select Grant access and require multifactor authentication, or choose an authentication strength if your organisation uses strengths.
Start in Report-only, review sign-in logs, then turn On.

steps: Before creating a new policy, check whether Defender already created an Intune security task for this exact recommendation.
In the Intune admin center, go to Endpoint security > Security tasks.

Open the task that matches "Block credential stealing from the Windows local security authority subsystem (lsass.exe)" if it exists, select Accept, and use the Defender-provided remediation text as the source of truth.
In the Intune admin center, go to Endpoint security > Attack surface reduction.
Select Create Policy.
Platform: Windows. Profile: Attack surface reduction rules. Click Create.
Name the policy "Secure Score ASR - Block credential stealing from the Windows local security authority subsystem (lsass.exe)". Click Next.
In Configuration settings, expand Attack Surface Reduction.
Set "Block credential stealing from the Windows local security authority subsystem (lsass.exe)" to Block. For a safer pilot, set it to Audit first, review events, then switch to Block.
Add per-rule exclusions only if a documented business app is blocked in testing. Keep exclusions narrow.
Assign to a pilot device group, then Review + create.
Open Group Policy Management Console (gpmc.msc).
Create or edit the approved ASR GPO.
Go to Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Microsoft Defender Antivirus > Microsoft Defender Exploit Guard > Attack Surface Reduction.
Open Configure Attack Surface Reduction rules.
Set the policy to Enabled.
Click Show.
Add Value name = 9e6c4e1f-7d60-472f-ba1a-a39ef669e4b2. Add Value = 1. Value 1 means Block.
Click OK, OK again, then link/security-filter the GPO to your pilot devices.

scripts.detection:
# Detection — exits 0 if compliant, 1 if not

# Detection example
$pref = Get-MpPreference
$ids = @($pref.AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids)
$actions = @($pref.AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions)
$index = [array]::IndexOf($ids, '9e6c4e1f-7d60-472f-ba1a-a39ef669e4b2')
if ($index -ge 0 -and $actions[$index] -eq 'Enabled') { exit 0 } else { exit 1 }

# Remediation example
Add-MpPreference -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids 9e6c4e1f-7d60-472f-ba1a-a39ef669e4b2 -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions Enabled

# Manual verification
Get-MpPreference | Select-Object AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids, AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions

# Detection script exits 0 on compliant devices.

steps: Open https://intune.microsoft.com.
Go to Endpoint security > Firewall.
Select Create Policy.
Platform: Windows 10 and later.
Profile: Microsoft Defender Firewall.
Name the policy: Secure Score - Secure Microsoft Defender firewall private profile.
Open Configuration settings.
Configure these settings: Private profile Firewall = True; Private profile Default inbound action = Block; Private profile Default outbound action = Allow.
Select Next until Assignments.
Assign to a pilot device group first, then Review + create.
After pilot success, assign to production device groups.
intune.value: Private profile enabled; default inbound Block; default outbound Allow

steps: Open Group Policy Management Console: gpmc.msc.
Create a new pilot GPO or edit your approved security baseline GPO.
Go to: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security > Windows Defender Firewall Properties.
Open the policy setting: Private Profile tab.
Set it to: Firewall state = On; Inbound connections = Block; Outbound connections = Allow.
Click OK/Apply.
Link the GPO only to a pilot OU first.
On a pilot device, run gpupdate /force or wait for normal policy refresh.
Reboot the pilot device if the setting requires a restart.
After testing, link the GPO to the wider production OU.

steps: Before creating a new policy, check whether Defender already created an Intune security task for this exact recommendation.
In the Intune admin center, go to Endpoint security > Security tasks.
Open the task that matches "Block Adobe Reader from creating child processes" if it exists, select Accept, and use the Defender-provided remediation text as the source of truth.
In the Intune admin center, go to Endpoint security > Attack surface reduction.
Select Create Policy.
Platform: Windows. Profile: Attack surface reduction rules. Click Create.
Name the policy "Secure Score ASR - Block Adobe Reader from creating child processes". Click Next.
In Configuration settings, expand Attack Surface Reduction.
Set "Process creation from Adobe Reader (beta)" to Block. For a safer pilot, set it to Audit first, review events, then switch to Block.
Add per-rule exclusions only if a documented business app is blocked in testing. Keep exclusions narrow.
Assign to a pilot device group, then Review + create.
Open Group Policy Management Console (gpmc.msc).
Create or edit the approved ASR GPO.
Go to Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Microsoft Defender Antivirus > Microsoft Defender Exploit Guard > Attack Surface Reduction.
Open Configure Attack Surface Reduction rules.
Set the policy to Enabled.
Click Show.
Add Value name = 7674ba52-37eb-4a4f-a9a1-f0f9a1619a2c. Add Value = 1. Value 1 means Block.
Click OK, OK again, then link/security-filter the GPO to your pilot devices.

gpo.setting_name: Configure Attack Surface Reduction rules

In the left navigation, open Exposure Management or Secure Score, then open Microsoft Secure Score.
Open Recommended actions and search for this exact title: "Update Microsoft Defender Antivirus definitions for Linux".
Click the recommendation row. Read At a glance, User impact, affected users/devices/entities, and the Implementation section before making any change.
If a Manage button is shown, click Manage to jump to the Microsoft configuration page for this recommendation.

If this is a Device recommendation, open the linked Microsoft Defender Vulnerability Management security recommendation. Device Secure Score actions are remediated from the linked recommendation rather than by manually setting Secure Score status.
Add a note in the recommendation with your change ticket number and planned rollout ring.
After implementation, return to the recommendation and check status. Secure Score points generally take time to refresh; do not mark a device recommendation as complete manually.
defender.notes: Use this for every recommendation to locate Microsoft's tenant-specific implementation text, affected objects, points, and validation status.

validation_steps: Check Intune per-setting status.
On a pilot device, run Get-MpPreference or Get-MpComputerStatus to verify the effective setting.
Return to Secure Score/Defender and verify after refresh.

result.summary: Start with the Defender Secure Score recommendation flyout. It contains the tenant-specific affected objects and Microsoft implementation text. Do not apply broad changes to all users/devices until a pilot succeeds.

steps: Open the Secure Score recommendation "Locate accounts in built-in Operator Groups" and check whether Microsoft lists a Group Policy path or Windows security baseline mapping.
If Microsoft lists a GPO path, open Group Policy Management Console and create a new pilot GPO instead of editing a broad production GPO immediately.
Configure only the exact setting and value Microsoft lists. Do not choose a similar-looking ADMX setting unless it is explicitly named.
Link the GPO to a pilot OU or apply security filtering to a pilot group.
Run gpupdate /force on one pilot device and confirm the effective setting with gpresult.
Expand the GPO only after the pilot device and Secure Score/Defender telemetry validate successfully.
gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings
gpo.setting_name: Locate accounts in built-in Operator Groups

steps: Open the Secure Score recommendation "Locate accounts in built-in Operator Groups" and confirm the recommendation actually has an Entra identity, role, Conditional Access, consent, or authentication component.
Use a least-privileged administrator role for the change. Avoid using Global Administrator unless the task requires it.
Identify the exact users, groups, roles, apps, or policies affected before making changes.
For Conditional Access, create or modify the policy in Report-only mode first and exclude approved emergency access accounts.
Review Entra sign-in logs or audit logs for pilot impact.
Turn the policy On or save the production setting only after pilot validation.
entra.notes: Conditional: applicable only when the finding involves Entra identities, hybrid privileged accounts, leaked credentials, or cloud roles.

defender.portal_path: Microsoft Defender portal > Secure Score or Defender for Identity security posture assessments
steps: Open Microsoft Defender portal > Secure Score or Defender for Identity security posture assessments.
Search/open the exact assessment: Locate accounts in built-in Operator Groups.
Open the affected entities list. Export or copy affected users, groups, computers, GPOs, certificate templates, servers, or domain controllers.
Read the Microsoft remediation text for that assessment before changing AD/AD CS. These recommendations are entity-specific.
Make the AD/Entra/identity change only to the affected entities named by the assessment.
For group membership findings, remove unnecessary privileged membership after owner approval.
For account attribute findings, correct the exact insecure attribute shown by Defender.
For stale/dormant accounts, disable/remove only after owner validation.
defender.notes: Use Defender for Identity assessment output. These are usually AD/AD CS/entity remediation tasks, not generic Intune policies.

validation_steps: gpresult shows the pilot GPO is applied.
The local effective setting matches the target value.
Secure Score/Defender status improves after telemetry refresh.

rollback.defender: Use your AD/AD CS backup/change process to reverse only the changed object/permission/policy.

steps: Open https://intune.microsoft.com.
Go to Devices > Configuration.
Select Create > New policy.
Platform: Windows 10 and later.
Profile type: Settings catalog.
Select Create.
Name the profile: Secure Score - Set default behavior for 'AutoRun' to 'Enabled: Do not execute any autorun commands'.
Select Add settings.
In the Settings picker search box, search for: Set the default behavior for AutoRun OR AutoPlay Policies.
Select the exact setting that matches the name in settings_to_configure. Do not select a similarly named setting from another product area.
Configure the setting value to: Enabled; Default AutoRun behavior = Do not execute any autorun commands.
Select Next until Assignments.
Assign only to your pilot device group first.
Select Review + create > Create.
After pilot success, expand the assignment to the production group.
intune.value: Enabled; Default AutoRun behavior = Do not execute any autorun commands

steps: Open Group Policy Management Console: gpmc.msc.
Create a new pilot GPO or edit your approved security baseline GPO.
Go to: Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > AutoPlay Policies.
Open the policy setting: Set the default behavior for AutoRun.
Set it to: Enabled; Default AutoRun behavior = Do not execute any autorun commands.
Click OK/Apply.
Link the GPO only to a pilot OU first.
On a pilot device, run gpupdate /force or wait for normal policy refresh.
Reboot the pilot device if the setting requires a restart.
After testing, link the GPO to the wider production OU.

steps: Before creating a new policy, check whether Defender already created an Intune security task for this exact recommendation.
In the Intune admin center, go to Endpoint security > Security tasks.
Open the task that matches "Disable 'Anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts'" if it exists, select Accept, and use the Defender-provided remediation text as the source of truth.
In the Intune admin center, go to Devices > Manage devices > Configuration > Create > New policy.
Select Platform = Windows 10 and later unless the recommendation is explicitly for another OS. Select Profile type = Settings catalog. Click Create.
On Basics, name the policy "Secure Score - 117 - Disable 'Anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts'". Click Next.
On Configuration settings, click Add settings. In Search, search for: Disable Anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts.
Add the matching Microsoft setting and configure it to: Disabled as stated in the recommendation. Do not pick a look-alike setting unless the Secure Score Implementation section confirms it.
Click Next. Assign first to a pilot device group. Do not assign to All devices for the first test unless your change process allows it.
Click Next through Scope tags and Applicability rules if not used. On Review + create, click Create.
Monitor deployment under Devices > Configuration > the policy > Device and user check-in status and Per-setting status.
Open Group Policy Management Console (gpmc.msc).
Create a new GPO for this recommendation or edit your approved security baseline GPO.
Go to: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options.
Open the setting: Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts.
Set it to: Enabled.
Link the GPO to a pilot OU or apply security filtering to a pilot group first.
On a pilot device, run gpupdate /force or wait for normal Group Policy refresh.

steps: Before creating a new policy, check whether Defender already created an Intune security task for this exact recommendation.
In the Intune admin center, go to Endpoint security > Security tasks.
Open the task that matches "Enable 'Safe DLL Search Mode'" if it exists, select Accept, and use the Defender-provided remediation text as the source of truth.
In the Intune admin center, go to Devices > Manage devices > Configuration > Create > New policy.
Select Platform = Windows 10 and later unless the recommendation is explicitly for another OS. Select Profile type = Settings catalog. Click Create.
On Basics, name the policy "Secure Score - 121 - Enable 'Safe DLL Search Mode'". Click Next.
On Configuration settings, click Add settings. In Search, search for: Enable Safe DLL Search Mode.

Add the matching Microsoft setting and configure it to: Enabled / enforced as stated in the recommendation. Do not pick a look-alike setting unless the Secure Score Implementation section confirms it.
Click Next. Assign first to a pilot device group. Do not assign to All devices for the first test unless your change process allows it.
Click Next through Scope tags and Applicability rules if not used. On Review + create, click Create.
Monitor deployment under Devices > Configuration > the policy > Device and user check-in status and Per-setting status.
Open Group Policy Management Console (gpmc.msc).
Create a new GPO for this recommendation or edit your approved security baseline GPO.
Go to: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options.
Open the setting: MSS: (SafeDllSearchMode) Enable Safe DLL search mode.
Set it to: Enabled.
Link the GPO to a pilot OU or apply security filtering to a pilot group first.
On a pilot device, run gpupdate /force or wait for normal Group Policy refresh.
gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options
gpo.setting_name: MSS: (SafeDllSearchMode) Enable Safe DLL search mode

steps: Open the Microsoft Defender portal at security.microsoft.com.

If this is a Device recommendation, open the linked Microsoft Defender Vulnerability Management security recommendation. Device Secure Score actions are remediated from the linked recommendation rather than by manually setting Secure Score status.
Add a note in the recommendation with your change ticket number and planned rollout ring.

steps: Open https://intune.microsoft.com.

Open the policy setting: Enable insecure guest logons.

Click OK/Apply.
Link the GPO only to a pilot OU first.

Reboot the pilot device if the setting requires a restart.
After testing, link the GPO to the wider production OU.

steps: Before creating a new policy, check whether Defender already created an Intune security task for this exact recommendation.

Select Platform = Windows 10 and later unless the recommendation is explicitly for another OS. Select Profile type = Settings catalog. Click Create.
On Basics, name the policy "Secure Score - 130 - Disable 'Always install with elevated privileges'". Click Next.
On Configuration settings, click Add settings. In Search, search for: Disable Always install with elevated privileges.
Add the matching Microsoft setting and configure it to: Disabled as stated in the recommendation. Do not pick a look-alike setting unless the Secure Score Implementation section confirms it.
Click Next. Assign first to a pilot device group. Do not assign to All devices for the first test unless your change process allows it.
Click Next through Scope tags and Applicability rules if not used. On Review + create, click Create.
Monitor deployment under Devices > Configuration > the policy > Device and user check-in status and Per-setting status.
Open Group Policy Management Console (gpmc.msc).
Create a new GPO for this recommendation or edit your approved security baseline GPO.
Go to: Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Windows Installer.
Open the setting: Always install with elevated privileges.
Set it to: Disabled.
Link the GPO to a pilot OU or apply security filtering to a pilot group first.
On a pilot device, run gpupdate /force or wait for normal Group Policy refresh.

scripts.detection:
# Detection — exits 0 if compliant, 1 if not

# Disable/remove SMBv1 client optional feature
Disable-WindowsOptionalFeature -Online -FeatureName SMB1Protocol-Client -NoRestart

# Fallback: disable SMBv1 mini-redirector service if Microsoft documentation for your OS uses service control
sc.exe config mrxsmb10 start= disabled
scripts.implementation: # Use the Intune steps above to implement this recommendation
scripts.validation:
# Run PowerShell: Get-WindowsOptionalFeature -Online -FeatureName SMB1Protocol-Client
# Run Command Prompt: sc.exe qc mrxsmb10

Use a least-privileged administrator role for the change. Avoid using Global Administrator unless the task requires it.
Identify the exact users, groups, roles, apps, or policies affected before making changes.
For Conditional Access, create or modify the policy in Report-only mode first and exclude approved emergency access accounts.
Review Entra sign-in logs or audit logs for pilot impact.
Turn the policy On or save the production setting only after pilot validation.
entra.notes: Conditional: Entra is relevant for admin roles/groups used to scope or administer the policy, but not the primary implementation plane.

steps: Open the Microsoft Defender portal at security.microsoft.com.

If a Manage button is shown, click Manage to jump to the Microsoft configuration page for this recommendation.

If this is a Device recommendation, open the linked Microsoft Defender Vulnerability Management security recommendation. Device Secure Score actions are remediated from the linked recommendation rather than by manually setting Secure Score status.
Add a note in the recommendation with your change ticket number and planned rollout ring.

gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings
gpo.setting_name: Enable Conditional Access policies to block legacy authentication

steps: Sign in to the Microsoft Entra admin center.
Go to Protection > Conditional Access > Policies > New policy.
Name it Secure Score - Block legacy authentication.
Under Users, include All users. Exclude emergency access accounts if required by your policy.
Under Target resources, choose All cloud apps.
Under Conditions > Client apps, configure the policy to target legacy authentication clients.
Under Grant, select Block access.
Set Enable policy to Report-only first, check sign-in logs for legacy-auth dependencies, then set to On.
entra.notes: Use Microsoft Entra admin center / Conditional Access / Identity Protection.

steps: Open the Secure Score recommendation "Reversible passwords found in GPOs" and check whether Microsoft lists a Group Policy path or Windows security baseline mapping.
If Microsoft lists a GPO path, open Group Policy Management Console and create a new pilot GPO instead of editing a broad production GPO immediately.
Configure only the exact setting and value Microsoft lists. Do not choose a similar-looking ADMX setting unless it is explicitly named.
Link the GPO to a pilot OU or apply security filtering to a pilot group.
Run gpupdate /force on one pilot device and confirm the effective setting with gpresult.
Expand the GPO only after the pilot device and Secure Score/Defender telemetry validate successfully.
gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings
gpo.setting_name: Reversible passwords found in GPOs

steps: Open the Secure Score recommendation "Reversible passwords found in GPOs" and confirm the recommendation actually has an Entra identity, role, Conditional Access, consent, or authentication component.
Use a least-privileged administrator role for the change. Avoid using Global Administrator unless the task requires it.
Identify the exact users, groups, roles, apps, or policies affected before making changes.
For Conditional Access, create or modify the policy in Report-only mode first and exclude approved emergency access accounts.
Review Entra sign-in logs or audit logs for pilot impact.
Turn the policy On or save the production setting only after pilot validation.
entra.notes: Conditional: applicable only when the finding involves Entra identities, hybrid privileged accounts, leaked credentials, or cloud roles.

defender.portal_path: Microsoft Defender portal > Secure Score or Defender for Identity security posture assessments
steps: Open Microsoft Defender portal > Secure Score or Defender for Identity security posture assessments.
Search/open the exact assessment: Reversible passwords found in GPOs.
Open the affected entities list. Export or copy affected users, groups, computers, GPOs, certificate templates, servers, or domain controllers.
Read the Microsoft remediation text for that assessment before changing AD/AD CS. These recommendations are entity-specific.
Make the AD/Entra/identity change only to the affected entities named by the assessment.
For group membership findings, remove unnecessary privileged membership after owner approval.
For account attribute findings, correct the exact insecure attribute shown by Defender.
For stale/dormant accounts, disable/remove only after owner validation.
defender.notes: Use Defender for Identity assessment output. These are usually AD/AD CS/entity remediation tasks, not generic Intune policies.

validation_steps: gpresult shows the pilot GPO is applied.
The local effective setting matches the target value.
Secure Score/Defender status improves after telemetry refresh.

rollback.intune: Remove the configuration policy or set to Not Configured in Intune
rollback.gpo: Set the policy to Not Configured in Group Policy Management
rollback.defender: Use your AD/AD CS backup/change process to reverse only the changed object/permission/policy.

risks: Start with the Defender Secure Score recommendation flyout. It contains the tenant-specific affected objects and Microsoft implementation text. Do not apply broad changes to all users/devices until a pilot succeeds.
Verify compatibility with existing policies before applying.

references.title: Secure Score: Reversible passwords found in GPOs
