rollback.defender: Use your AD/AD CS backup/change process to reverse only the changed object/permission/policy.

result.summary: Start with the Defender Secure Score recommendation flyout. It contains the tenant-specific affected objects and Microsoft implementation text. Do not apply broad changes to all users/devices until a pilot succeeds.

steps: Open the Secure Score recommendation "Remove discoverable passwords in Active Directory account attributes" and check whether Microsoft lists a Group Policy path or Windows security baseline mapping.
If Microsoft lists a GPO path, open Group Policy Management Console and create a new pilot GPO instead of editing a broad production GPO immediately.
Configure only the exact setting and value Microsoft lists. Do not choose a similar-looking ADMX setting unless it is explicitly named.
Link the GPO to a pilot OU or apply security filtering to a pilot group.
Run gpupdate /force on one pilot device and confirm the effective setting with gpresult.
Expand the GPO only after the pilot device and Secure Score/Defender telemetry validate successfully.
gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings
gpo.setting_name: Remove discoverable passwords in Active Directory account attributes

steps: Open the Secure Score recommendation "Remove discoverable passwords in Active Directory account attributes" and confirm the recommendation actually has an Entra identity, role, Conditional Access, consent, or authentication component.
Use a least-privileged administrator role for the change. Avoid using Global Administrator unless the task requires it.
Identify the exact users, groups, roles, apps, or policies affected before making changes.
For Conditional Access, create or modify the policy in Report-only mode first and exclude approved emergency access accounts.
Review Entra sign-in logs or audit logs for pilot impact.
Turn the policy On or save the production setting only after pilot validation.
entra.notes: Conditional: applicable only when the finding involves Entra identities, hybrid privileged accounts, leaked credentials, or cloud roles.

defender.portal_path: Microsoft Defender portal > Secure Score or Defender for Identity security posture assessments
steps: Open Microsoft Defender portal > Secure Score or Defender for Identity security posture assessments.
Search/open the exact assessment: Remove discoverable passwords in Active Directory account attributes.
Open the affected entities list. Export or copy affected users, groups, computers, GPOs, certificate templates, servers, or domain controllers.
Read the Microsoft remediation text for that assessment before changing AD/AD CS. These recommendations are entity-specific.
Make the AD/Entra/identity change only to the affected entities named by the assessment.
For group membership findings, remove unnecessary privileged membership after owner approval.
For account attribute findings, correct the exact insecure attribute shown by Defender.
For stale/dormant accounts, disable/remove only after owner validation.
defender.notes: Use Defender for Identity assessment output. These are usually AD/AD CS/entity remediation tasks, not generic Intune policies.

validation_steps: gpresult shows the pilot GPO is applied.
The local effective setting matches the target value.
Secure Score/Defender status improves after telemetry refresh.

rollback.defender: Use your AD/AD CS backup/change process to reverse only the changed object/permission/policy.

steps: Before creating a new policy, check whether Defender already created an Intune security task for this exact recommendation.
In the Intune admin center, go to Endpoint security > Security tasks.

Open the task that matches "Enable 'Local Security Authority (LSA) protection' on Windows 11 22h2 and higher" if it exists, select Accept, and use the Defender-provided remediation text as the source of truth.
In the Intune admin center, go to Devices > Manage devices > Configuration > Create > New policy.
Select Platform = Windows 10 and later unless the recommendation is explicitly for another OS. Select Profile type = Settings catalog. Click Create.
On Basics, name the policy "Secure Score - 147 - Enable 'Local Security Authority (LSA) protection' on Windows 11 22h2 and higher". Click Next.
On Configuration settings, click Add settings. In Search, search for: Local Security Authority protection, Configure LSASS to run as a protected process.
Add the matching Microsoft setting and configure it to: Enabled. Do not pick a look-alike setting unless the Secure Score Implementation section confirms it.
Click Next. Assign first to a pilot device group. Do not assign to All devices for the first test unless your change process allows it.
Click Next through Scope tags and Applicability rules if not used. On Review + create, click Create.
Monitor deployment under Devices > Configuration > the policy > Device and user check-in status and Per-setting status.

Open the Secure Score recommendation "Enable 'Local Security Authority (LSA) protection' on Windows 11 22h2 and higher" and check whether Microsoft lists a Group Policy path or Windows security baseline mapping.
If Microsoft lists a GPO path, open Group Policy Management Console and create a new pilot GPO instead of editing a broad production GPO immediately.
Configure only the exact setting and value Microsoft lists. Do not choose a similar-looking ADMX setting unless it is explicitly named.
Link the GPO to a pilot OU or apply security filtering to a pilot group.
Run gpupdate /force on one pilot device and confirm the effective setting with gpresult.
Expand the GPO only after the pilot device and Secure Score/Defender telemetry validate successfully.
gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings
gpo.setting_name: Enable 'Local Security Authority (LSA) protection' on Windows 11 22h2 and higher

steps: Open the Microsoft Defender portal at security.microsoft.com.
In the left navigation, open Exposure Management or Secure Score, then open Microsoft Secure Score.
Open Recommended actions and search for this exact title: "Enable 'Local Security Authority (LSA) protection' on Windows 11 22h2 and higher".
Click the recommendation row. Read At a glance, User impact, affected users/devices/entities, and the Implementation section before making any change.
If a Manage button is shown, click Manage to jump to the Microsoft configuration page for this recommendation.

If this is a Device recommendation, open the linked Microsoft Defender Vulnerability Management security recommendation. Device Secure Score actions are remediated from the linked recommendation rather than by manually setting Secure Score status.
Add a note in the recommendation with your change ticket number and planned rollout ring.
After implementation, return to the recommendation and check status. Secure Score points generally take time to refresh; do not mark a device recommendation as complete manually.
defender.notes: Use this for every recommendation to locate Microsoft's tenant-specific implementation text, affected objects, points, and validation status.

validation_steps: Verify the Intune policy shows Succeeded for pilot devices.
On a pilot device, confirm the local policy/effective configuration changed.
Return to Defender/Secure Score and verify the recommendation status after refresh.

steps: Open https://intune.microsoft.com.
Go to Devices > Configuration.
Select Create > New policy.
Platform: Windows 10 and later.
Profile type: Settings catalog.
Select Create.
Name the profile: Secure Score - SMB server security hardening against authentication relay attacks.
Select Add settings.
In the Settings picker search box, search for: Microsoft network server: Digitally sign communications (always) OR SMB server signing required.
Select the exact setting that matches the name in settings_to_configure. Do not select a similarly named setting from another product area.
Configure the setting value to: Enabled / required.
Select Next until Assignments.
Assign only to your pilot device group first.
Select Review + create > Create.
After pilot success, expand the assignment to the production group.
Open Group Policy Management Console: gpmc.msc.
Create a new pilot GPO or edit your approved security baseline GPO.
Go to: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options.
Open the policy setting: Microsoft network server: Digitally sign communications (always).
Set it to: Enabled.
Click OK/Apply.
Link the GPO only to a pilot OU first.
On a pilot device, run gpupdate /force or wait for normal policy refresh.
Reboot the pilot device if the setting requires a restart.
After testing, link the GPO to the wider production OU.

steps: Before creating a new policy, check whether Defender already created an Intune security task for this exact recommendation.
In the Intune admin center, go to Endpoint security > Security tasks.
Open the task that matches "Set 'Account lockout duration' to 15 minutes or more" if it exists, select Accept, and use the Defender-provided remediation text as the source of truth.
In the Intune admin center, go to Devices > Manage devices > Configuration > Create > New policy.
Select Platform = Windows 10 and later unless the recommendation is explicitly for another OS. Select Profile type = Settings catalog. Click Create.
On Basics, name the policy "Secure Score - 155 - Set 'Account lockout duration' to 15 minutes or more". Click Next.
On Configuration settings, click Add settings. In Search, search for: Set Account lockout duration to 15 minutes or more.
Add the matching Microsoft setting and configure it to: 15 minutes or more. Do not pick a look-alike setting unless the Secure Score Implementation section confirms it.
Click Next. Assign first to a pilot device group. Do not assign to All devices for the first test unless your change process allows it.
Click Next through Scope tags and Applicability rules if not used. On Review + create, click Create.
Monitor deployment under Devices > Configuration > the policy > Device and user check-in status and Per-setting status.
Open Group Policy Management Console (gpmc.msc).
Create a new GPO for this recommendation or edit your approved security baseline GPO.
Go to: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Account Policies > Account Lockout Policy.
Open the setting: Account lockout duration.
Set it to: 15 minutes or more.
Link the GPO to a pilot OU or apply security filtering to a pilot group first.
On a pilot device, run gpupdate /force or wait for normal Group Policy refresh.

gpo.setting_name: Ensure password protection is enabled for on-prem Active Directory

steps: Plan which domain controllers will receive the Microsoft Entra Password Protection DC agent and which server will host the proxy service.
Install the Microsoft Entra Password Protection proxy service on a supported member server.
Register the proxy service with Microsoft Entra ID using the documented registration process.
Install the Microsoft Entra Password Protection DC agent on each domain controller.
In the Entra admin center, configure Password protection and start in Audit mode if this is your first rollout.
After reviewing events and resolving false positives, switch the mode to Enforced.
entra.notes: Use Microsoft Entra admin center / Conditional Access / Identity Protection.

defender.portal_path: Microsoft Defender portal > Secure Score or Defender for Identity security posture assessments
steps: Open Microsoft Defender portal > Secure Score or Defender for Identity security posture assessments.
Search/open the exact assessment: Rotate old password for sMSA and set up valid rotation interval in the GPO.
Open the affected entities list. Export or copy affected users, groups, computers, GPOs, certificate templates, servers, or domain controllers.
Read the Microsoft remediation text for that assessment before changing AD/AD CS. These recommendations are entity-specific.
Make the AD/Entra/identity change only to the affected entities named by the assessment.
For group membership findings, remove unnecessary privileged membership after owner approval.
For account attribute findings, correct the exact insecure attribute shown by Defender.
For stale/dormant accounts, disable/remove only after owner validation.

Use a least-privileged administrator role for the change. Avoid using Global Administrator unless the task requires it.
Identify the exact users, groups, roles, apps, or policies affected before making changes.
For Conditional Access, create or modify the policy in Report-only mode first and exclude approved emergency access accounts.
Review Entra sign-in logs or audit logs for pilot impact.
Turn the policy On or save the production setting only after pilot validation.
entra.notes: Conditional: Entra is relevant for admin roles/groups used to scope or administer the policy, but not the primary implementation plane.

steps: Open the Microsoft Defender portal at security.microsoft.com.
In the left navigation, open Exposure Management or Secure Score, then open Microsoft Secure Score.
Open Recommended actions and search for this exact title: "Turn on Microsoft Defender for Office 365 in SharePoint, OneDrive, and Microsoft Teams".
Click the recommendation row. Read At a glance, User impact, affected users/devices/entities, and the Implementation section before making any change.
If a Manage button is shown, click Manage to jump to the Microsoft configuration page for this recommendation.

If this is a Device recommendation, open the linked Microsoft Defender Vulnerability Management security recommendation. Device Secure Score actions are remediated from the linked recommendation rather than by manually setting Secure Score status.
Add a note in the recommendation with your change ticket number and planned rollout ring.
After implementation, return to the recommendation and check status. Secure Score points generally take time to refresh; do not mark a device recommendation as complete manually.
defender.notes: Use this for every recommendation to locate Microsoft's tenant-specific implementation text, affected objects, points, and validation status.

validation_steps: Confirm the Secure Score recommendation no longer shows exposed users/devices/entities or that Microsoft shows the action as completed/points awarded.
For device recommendations, verify affected device count in Defender Vulnerability Management after telemetry refresh.

rollback.intune: Remove the configuration policy or set to Not Configured in Intune
rollback.gpo: Set the policy to Not Configured in Group Policy Management

risks: Start with the Defender Secure Score recommendation flyout. It contains the tenant-specific affected objects and Microsoft implementation text. Do not apply broad changes to all users/devices until a pilot succeeds.
Verify compatibility with existing policies before applying.

references.title: Secure Score: Turn on Microsoft Defender for Office 365 in SharePoint, OneDrive, and Microsoft Teams

steps: Before creating a new policy, check whether Defender already created an Intune security task for this exact recommendation.
In the Intune admin center, go to Endpoint security > Security tasks.
Open the task that matches "Enable Microsoft Defender Antivirus real-time behavior monitoring" if it exists, select Accept, and use the Defender-provided remediation text as the source of truth.
In Intune admin center, go to Endpoint security > Antivirus > Create Policy.
Platform: Windows. Profile: Microsoft Defender Antivirus. Click Create.
In Configuration settings, find "Turn on behavior monitoring" and set it to "Enabled".
Assign to a pilot group first, then Review + create.
Open GPMC and edit the target GPO.
Go to Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Microsoft Defender Antivirus.
Open the relevant section for "Turn on behavior monitoring". Configure the policy to "Enabled".
Apply to pilot OU/security group and validate with Get-MpPreference/Get-MpComputerStatus.

steps: Open https://intune.microsoft.com.
Go to Devices > Configuration.
Select Create > New policy.
Platform: Windows 10 and later.
Profile type: Settings catalog.
Select Create.
Name the profile: Secure Score - Disable 'Autoplay for non-volume devices'.
Select Add settings.
In the Settings picker search box, search for: Disallow Autoplay for non-volume devices OR AutoPlay Policies.
Select the exact setting that matches the name in settings_to_configure. Do not select a similarly named setting from another product area.
Configure the setting value to: Enabled.
Select Next until Assignments.
Assign only to your pilot device group first.
Select Review + create > Create.
After pilot success, expand the assignment to the production group.
Open Group Policy Management Console: gpmc.msc.
Create a new pilot GPO or edit your approved security baseline GPO.
Go to: Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > AutoPlay Policies.
Open the policy setting: Disallow Autoplay for non-volume devices.
Set it to: Enabled.
Click OK/Apply.
Link the GPO only to a pilot OU first.
On a pilot device, run gpupdate /force or wait for normal policy refresh.
Reboot the pilot device if the setting requires a restart.
After testing, link the GPO to the wider production OU.

steps: Before creating a new policy, check whether Defender already created an Intune security task for this exact recommendation.
In the Intune admin center, go to Endpoint security > Security tasks.
Open the task that matches "Disable sending unencrypted password to third-party SMB servers" if it exists, select Accept, and use the Defender-provided remediation text as the source of truth.
In the Intune admin center, go to Devices > Manage devices > Configuration > Create > New policy.
Select Platform = Windows 10 and later unless the recommendation is explicitly for another OS. Select Profile type = Settings catalog. Click Create.
On Basics, name the policy "Secure Score - 168 - Disable sending unencrypted password to third-party SMB servers". Click Next.
On Configuration settings, click Add settings. In Search, search for: Disable sending unencrypted password to third-party SMB servers.
Add the matching Microsoft setting and configure it to: third-party SMB servers. Do not pick a look-alike setting unless the Secure Score Implementation section confirms it.
Click Next. Assign first to a pilot device group. Do not assign to All devices for the first test unless your change process allows it.
Click Next through Scope tags and Applicability rules if not used. On Review + create, click Create.
Monitor deployment under Devices > Configuration > the policy > Device and user check-in status and Per-setting status.
Open the Secure Score recommendation and check whether Microsoft lists a GPO path or Windows security baseline setting.
If Microsoft lists a GPO path, create/edit a pilot GPO and configure only that exact setting/value.
If no GPO path is listed, do not guess. Use Intune Settings Catalog, Defender security task, or the product-specific method instead.

steps: Open https://intune.microsoft.com.
Go to Devices > Configuration.
Select Create > New policy.
Platform: Windows 10 and later.
Profile type: Settings catalog.
Select Create.
Name the profile: Secure Score - Enable Explorer Data Execution Prevention (DEP).
Select Add settings.
In the Settings picker search box, search for: Turn off Data Execution Prevention for Explorer.
Select the exact setting that matches the name in settings_to_configure. Do not select a similarly named setting from another product area.
Configure the setting value to: Disabled.
Select Next until Assignments.
Assign only to your pilot device group first.
Select Review + create > Create.
After pilot success, expand the assignment to the production group.
Open Group Policy Management Console: gpmc.msc.
Create a new pilot GPO or edit your approved security baseline GPO.
Go to: Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > File Explorer.
Open the policy setting: Turn off Data Execution Prevention for Explorer.
Set it to: Disabled.
Click OK/Apply.
Link the GPO only to a pilot OU first.
On a pilot device, run gpupdate /force or wait for normal policy refresh.
Reboot the pilot device if the setting requires a restart.
After testing, link the GPO to the wider production OU.

Click OK/Apply.
Link the GPO only to a pilot OU first.
On a pilot device, run gpupdate /force or wait for normal policy refresh.
Reboot the pilot device if the setting requires a restart.
After testing, link the GPO to the wider production OU.

steps: Open the Microsoft Defender portal at security.microsoft.com.
In the left navigation, open Exposure Management or Secure Score, then open Microsoft Secure Score.
Open Recommended actions and search for this exact title: "Enable Automatic Updates".
Click the recommendation row. Read At a glance, User impact, affected users/devices/entities, and the Implementation section before making any change.
If a Manage button is shown, click Manage to jump to the Microsoft configuration page for this recommendation.

If this is a Device recommendation, open the linked Microsoft Defender Vulnerability Management security recommendation. Device Secure Score actions are remediated from the linked recommendation rather than by manually setting Secure Score status.
Add a note in the recommendation with your change ticket number and planned rollout ring.
After implementation, return to the recommendation and check status. Secure Score points generally take time to refresh; do not mark a device recommendation as complete manually.

steps: Before creating a new policy, check whether Defender already created an Intune security task for this exact recommendation.
In the Intune admin center, go to Endpoint security > Security tasks.

Open the task that matches "Enable 'Domain member: Digitally encrypt or sign secure channel data (always)'" if it exists, select Accept, and use the Defender-provided remediation text as the source of truth.
In the Intune admin center, go to Devices > Manage devices > Configuration > Create > New policy.
Select Platform = Windows 10 and later unless the recommendation is explicitly for another OS. Select Profile type = Settings catalog. Click Create.
On Basics, name the policy "Secure Score - 179 - Enable 'Domain member: Digitally encrypt or sign secure channel data (always)'". Click Next.
On Configuration settings, click Add settings. In Search, search for: Enable Domain member: Digitally encrypt or sign secure channel data (always).

Add the matching Microsoft setting and configure it to: Enabled / enforced as stated in the recommendation. Do not pick a look-alike setting unless the Secure Score Implementation section confirms it.
Click Next. Assign first to a pilot device group. Do not assign to All devices for the first test unless your change process allows it.
Click Next through Scope tags and Applicability rules if not used. On Review + create, click Create.
Monitor deployment under Devices > Configuration > the policy > Device and user check-in status and Per-setting status.
Open Group Policy Management Console (gpmc.msc).
Create a new GPO for this recommendation or edit your approved security baseline GPO.
Go to: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options.
Open the setting: Domain member: Digitally encrypt or sign secure channel data (always).
Set it to: Enabled.
Link the GPO to a pilot OU or apply security filtering to a pilot group first.
On a pilot device, run gpupdate /force or wait for normal Group Policy refresh.

Click Next. Assign first to a pilot device group. Do not assign to All devices for the first test unless your change process allows it.
Click Next through Scope tags and Applicability rules if not used. On Review + create, click Create.
Monitor deployment under Devices > Configuration > the policy > Device and user check-in status and Per-setting status.
Open Group Policy Management Console (gpmc.msc).
Create a new GPO for this recommendation or edit your approved security baseline GPO.
Go to: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Account Policies > Password Policy.
Open the setting: Maximum password age.
Set it to: 60 days or fewer, but not 0.
Link the GPO to a pilot OU or apply security filtering to a pilot group first.
On a pilot device, run gpupdate /force or wait for normal Group Policy refresh.

gpo.setting_name: Maximum password age

steps: Open the Microsoft Defender portal at security.microsoft.com.
In the left navigation, open Exposure Management or Secure Score, then open Microsoft Secure Score.
Open Recommended actions and search for this exact title: "Set 'Maximum password age' to '60 or fewer days, but not 0'".
Click the recommendation row. Read At a glance, User impact, affected users/devices/entities, and the Implementation section before making any change.
If a Manage button is shown, click Manage to jump to the Microsoft configuration page for this recommendation.

If this is a Device recommendation, open the linked Microsoft Defender Vulnerability Management security recommendation. Device Secure Score actions are remediated from the linked recommendation rather than by manually setting Secure Score status.
Add a note in the recommendation with your change ticket number and planned rollout ring.
After implementation, return to the recommendation and check status. Secure Score points generally take time to refresh; do not mark a device recommendation as complete manually.

steps: Open the Secure Score recommendation "Stop clear text credentials exposure" and check whether Microsoft lists a Group Policy path or Windows security baseline mapping.
If Microsoft lists a GPO path, open Group Policy Management Console and create a new pilot GPO instead of editing a broad production GPO immediately.
Configure only the exact setting and value Microsoft lists. Do not choose a similar-looking ADMX setting unless it is explicitly named.
Link the GPO to a pilot OU or apply security filtering to a pilot group.
Run gpupdate /force on one pilot device and confirm the effective setting with gpresult.
Expand the GPO only after the pilot device and Secure Score/Defender telemetry validate successfully.
gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings
gpo.setting_name: Stop clear text credentials exposure

steps: Open the Secure Score recommendation "Stop clear text credentials exposure" and confirm the recommendation actually has an Entra identity, role, Conditional Access, consent, or authentication component.
Use a least-privileged administrator role for the change. Avoid using Global Administrator unless the task requires it.
Identify the exact users, groups, roles, apps, or policies affected before making changes.
For Conditional Access, create or modify the policy in Report-only mode first and exclude approved emergency access accounts.
Review Entra sign-in logs or audit logs for pilot impact.
Turn the policy On or save the production setting only after pilot validation.
entra.notes: Conditional: applicable only when the finding involves Entra identities, hybrid privileged accounts, leaked credentials, or cloud roles.

defender.portal_path: Microsoft Defender portal > Secure Score or Defender for Identity security posture assessments
steps: Open Microsoft Defender portal > Secure Score or Defender for Identity security posture assessments.
Search/open the exact assessment: Stop clear text credentials exposure.
Open the affected entities list. Export or copy affected users, groups, computers, GPOs, certificate templates, servers, or domain controllers.
Read the Microsoft remediation text for that assessment before changing AD/AD CS. These recommendations are entity-specific.
Make the AD/Entra/identity change only to the affected entities named by the assessment.
For group membership findings, remove unnecessary privileged membership after owner approval.
For account attribute findings, correct the exact insecure attribute shown by Defender.
For stale/dormant accounts, disable/remove only after owner validation.
defender.notes: Use Defender for Identity assessment output. These are usually AD/AD CS/entity remediation tasks, not generic Intune policies.

validation_steps: gpresult shows the pilot GPO is applied.
The local effective setting matches the target value.
Secure Score/Defender status improves after telemetry refresh.

rollback.intune: Remove the configuration policy or set to Not Configured in Intune
rollback.gpo: Set the policy to Not Configured in Group Policy Management
rollback.defender: Use your AD/AD CS backup/change process to reverse only the changed object/permission/policy.

Configure only the exact setting and value Microsoft lists. Do not choose a similar-looking ADMX setting unless it is explicitly named.
Link the GPO to a pilot OU or apply security filtering to a pilot group.
Run gpupdate /force on one pilot device and confirm the effective setting with gpresult.
Expand the GPO only after the pilot device and Secure Score/Defender telemetry validate successfully.
gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings
gpo.setting_name: Remove unsecure SID history attributes from entities

steps: Open the Secure Score recommendation "Remove unsecure SID history attributes from entities" and confirm the recommendation actually has an Entra identity, role, Conditional Access, consent, or authentication component.
Use a least-privileged administrator role for the change. Avoid using Global Administrator unless the task requires it.
Identify the exact users, groups, roles, apps, or policies affected before making changes.
For Conditional Access, create or modify the policy in Report-only mode first and exclude approved emergency access accounts.
Review Entra sign-in logs or audit logs for pilot impact.
Turn the policy On or save the production setting only after pilot validation.
entra.notes: Conditional: applicable only when the finding involves Entra identities, hybrid privileged accounts, leaked credentials, or cloud roles.

defender.portal_path: Microsoft Defender portal > Secure Score or Defender for Identity security posture assessments
steps: Open Microsoft Defender portal > Secure Score or Defender for Identity security posture assessments.
Search/open the exact assessment: Remove unsecure SID history attributes from entities.
Open the affected entities list. Export or copy affected users, groups, computers, GPOs, certificate templates, servers, or domain controllers.
Read the Microsoft remediation text for that assessment before changing AD/AD CS. These recommendations are entity-specific.
Make the AD/Entra/identity change only to the affected entities named by the assessment.
For group membership findings, remove unnecessary privileged membership after owner approval.
For account attribute findings, correct the exact insecure attribute shown by Defender.
For stale/dormant accounts, disable/remove only after owner validation.
defender.notes: Use Defender for Identity assessment output. These are usually AD/AD CS/entity remediation tasks, not generic Intune policies.

validation_steps: gpresult shows the pilot GPO is applied.
The local effective setting matches the target value.
Secure Score/Defender status improves after telemetry refresh.

rollback.gpo: Set the policy to Not Configured in Group Policy Management
rollback.defender: Use your AD/AD CS backup/change process to reverse only the changed object/permission/policy.

risks: Start with the Defender Secure Score recommendation flyout. It contains the tenant-specific affected objects and Microsoft implementation text. Do not apply broad changes to all users/devices until a pilot succeeds.

steps: Open the Microsoft Defender portal at security.microsoft.com.
In the left navigation, open Exposure Management or Secure Score, then open Microsoft Secure Score.
Open Recommended actions and search for this exact title: "Ensure 'External sharing' of calendars is not available".
Click the recommendation row. Read At a glance, User impact, affected users/devices/entities, and the Implementation section before making any change.
If a Manage button is shown, click Manage to jump to the Microsoft configuration page for this recommendation.

If this is a Device recommendation, open the linked Microsoft Defender Vulnerability Management security recommendation. Device Secure Score actions are remediated from the linked recommendation rather than by manually setting Secure Score status.
Add a note in the recommendation with your change ticket number and planned rollout ring.
After implementation, return to the recommendation and check status. Secure Score points generally take time to refresh; do not mark a device recommendation as complete manually.
defender.notes: Use this for every recommendation to locate Microsoft's tenant-specific implementation text, affected objects, points, and validation status.

validation_steps: Confirm the Secure Score recommendation no longer shows exposed users/devices/entities or that Microsoft shows the action as completed/points awarded.
For device recommendations, verify affected device count in Defender Vulnerability Management after telemetry refresh.

gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings
gpo.setting_name: Ensure the admin consent workflow is enabled

steps: Open Entra admin center.
Search/open the Secure Score recommendation: Ensure the admin consent workflow is enabled.
Click Manage if available or navigate to the Entra area named by the recommendation.
Configure the exact target state shown in the Secure Score Implementation section.
Pilot/report-only first for Conditional Access changes, then enforce.
entra.notes: Use Microsoft Entra admin center. Exact page/settings must be confirmed in the Secure Score implementation flyout for this title.

steps: Open the Microsoft Defender portal at security.microsoft.com.
In the left navigation, open Exposure Management or Secure Score, then open Microsoft Secure Score.
Open Recommended actions and search for this exact title: "Ensure the admin consent workflow is enabled".
Click the recommendation row. Read At a glance, User impact, affected users/devices/entities, and the Implementation section before making any change.
If a Manage button is shown, click Manage to jump to the Microsoft configuration page for this recommendation.

If this is a Device recommendation, open the linked Microsoft Defender Vulnerability Management security recommendation. Device Secure Score actions are remediated from the linked recommendation rather than by manually setting Secure Score status.
Add a note in the recommendation with your change ticket number and planned rollout ring.
After implementation, return to the recommendation and check status. Secure Score points generally take time to refresh; do not mark a device recommendation as complete manually.
defender.notes: Use this for every recommendation to locate Microsoft's tenant-specific implementation text, affected objects, points, and validation status.

validation_steps: Confirm the Secure Score recommendation no longer shows exposed users/devices/entities or that Microsoft shows the action as completed/points awarded.
For device recommendations, verify affected device count in Defender Vulnerability Management after telemetry refresh.

gpo.setting_name: Ensure Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps is enabled and configured

steps: Open the Secure Score recommendation "Ensure Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps is enabled and configured" and confirm the recommendation actually has an Entra identity, role, Conditional Access, consent, or authentication component.
Use a least-privileged administrator role for the change. Avoid using Global Administrator unless the task requires it.
Identify the exact users, groups, roles, apps, or policies affected before making changes.
For Conditional Access, create or modify the policy in Report-only mode first and exclude approved emergency access accounts.
Review Entra sign-in logs or audit logs for pilot impact.
Turn the policy On or save the production setting only after pilot validation.
entra.notes: Conditional: Entra is relevant for admin roles/groups used to scope or administer the policy, but not the primary implementation plane.

gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings
gpo.setting_name: Ensure that password hash sync is enabled for hybrid deployments

steps: Open Microsoft Entra Connect on the server that runs your sync configuration.
Select Configure.
Choose Customize synchronization options.
Sign in with the required Entra and AD DS permissions.
On Optional features, select Password hash synchronization.
Complete the wizard and allow synchronization to run.
Confirm the configuration in Microsoft Entra Connect Health or by reviewing sync status.

result.summary: Start with the Defender Secure Score recommendation flyout. It contains the tenant-specific affected objects and Microsoft implementation text. Do not apply broad changes to all users/devices until a pilot succeeds.

steps: Open the Secure Score recommendation "Remove access rights on suspicious accounts with the Admin SDHolder permission" and check whether Microsoft lists a Group Policy path or Windows security baseline mapping.
If Microsoft lists a GPO path, open Group Policy Management Console and create a new pilot GPO instead of editing a broad production GPO immediately.
Configure only the exact setting and value Microsoft lists. Do not choose a similar-looking ADMX setting unless it is explicitly named.
Link the GPO to a pilot OU or apply security filtering to a pilot group.
Run gpupdate /force on one pilot device and confirm the effective setting with gpresult.
Expand the GPO only after the pilot device and Secure Score/Defender telemetry validate successfully.
gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings
gpo.setting_name: Remove access rights on suspicious accounts with the Admin SDHolder permission

steps: Open the Secure Score recommendation "Remove access rights on suspicious accounts with the Admin SDHolder permission" and confirm the recommendation actually has an Entra identity, role, Conditional Access, consent, or authentication component.
Use a least-privileged administrator role for the change. Avoid using Global Administrator unless the task requires it.
Identify the exact users, groups, roles, apps, or policies affected before making changes.
For Conditional Access, create or modify the policy in Report-only mode first and exclude approved emergency access accounts.
Review Entra sign-in logs or audit logs for pilot impact.
Turn the policy On or save the production setting only after pilot validation.
entra.notes: Conditional: applicable only when the finding involves Entra identities, hybrid privileged accounts, leaked credentials, or cloud roles.

defender.portal_path: Microsoft Defender portal > Secure Score or Defender for Identity security posture assessments
steps: Open Microsoft Defender portal > Secure Score or Defender for Identity security posture assessments.
Search/open the exact assessment: Remove access rights on suspicious accounts with the Admin SDHolder permission.
Open the affected entities list. Export or copy affected users, groups, computers, GPOs, certificate templates, servers, or domain controllers.
Read the Microsoft remediation text for that assessment before changing AD/AD CS. These recommendations are entity-specific.
Make the AD/Entra/identity change only to the affected entities named by the assessment.
For group membership findings, remove unnecessary privileged membership after owner approval.
For account attribute findings, correct the exact insecure attribute shown by Defender.
For stale/dormant accounts, disable/remove only after owner validation.
defender.notes: Use Defender for Identity assessment output. These are usually AD/AD CS/entity remediation tasks, not generic Intune policies.

validation_steps: gpresult shows the pilot GPO is applied.
The local effective setting matches the target value.
Secure Score/Defender status improves after telemetry refresh.

rollback.intune: Remove the configuration policy or set to Not Configured in Intune
rollback.gpo: Set the policy to Not Configured in Group Policy Management
rollback.defender: Use your AD/AD CS backup/change process to reverse only the changed object/permission/policy.

risks: Start with the Defender Secure Score recommendation flyout. It contains the tenant-specific affected objects and Microsoft implementation text. Do not apply broad changes to all users/devices until a pilot succeeds.
Verify compatibility with existing policies before applying.

references.title: Secure Score: Remove access rights on suspicious accounts with the Admin SDHolder permission

Search/open the exact assessment: Edit misconfigured certificate templates ACL (ESC4).
Open the affected entities list. Export or copy affected users, groups, computers, GPOs, certificate templates, servers, or domain controllers.
Read the Microsoft remediation text for that assessment before changing AD/AD CS. These recommendations are entity-specific.
On the AD CS server/management workstation, open the affected certificate template or certificate authority named by the assessment.
Correct only the vulnerable ACL, owner, EKU, enrollment agent, web enrollment, RPC encryption, or arbitrary subject/application policy setting identified by Defender.
Do not change unrelated templates or CAs.
Restart/republish only if Microsoft or your AD CS change process requires it.
defender.notes: Use Defender for Identity assessment output. These are usually AD/AD CS/entity remediation tasks, not generic Intune policies.

validation_steps: gpresult shows the pilot GPO is applied.
The local effective setting matches the target value.
Secure Score/Defender status improves after telemetry refresh.

rollback.intune: Remove the configuration policy or set to Not Configured in Intune
rollback.gpo: Set the policy to Not Configured in Group Policy Management
rollback.defender: Use your AD/AD CS backup/change process to reverse only the changed object/permission/policy.

risks: Start with the Defender Secure Score recommendation flyout. It contains the tenant-specific affected objects and Microsoft implementation text. Do not apply broad changes to all users/devices until a pilot succeeds.
Verify compatibility with existing policies before applying.

references.title: Secure Score: Edit misconfigured certificate templates ACL (ESC4)

steps: Open the Secure Score recommendation "Edit misconfigured enrollment agent certificate template (ESC3)" and check whether Microsoft lists a Group Policy path or Windows security baseline mapping.
If Microsoft lists a GPO path, open Group Policy Management Console and create a new pilot GPO instead of editing a broad production GPO immediately.
Configure only the exact setting and value Microsoft lists. Do not choose a similar-looking ADMX setting unless it is explicitly named.
Link the GPO to a pilot OU or apply security filtering to a pilot group.
Run gpupdate /force on one pilot device and confirm the effective setting with gpresult.
Expand the GPO only after the pilot device and Secure Score/Defender telemetry validate successfully.
gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings
gpo.setting_name: Edit misconfigured enrollment agent certificate template (ESC3)

steps: Open the Secure Score recommendation "Edit misconfigured enrollment agent certificate template (ESC3)" and confirm the recommendation actually has an Entra identity, role, Conditional Access, consent, or authentication component.
Use a least-privileged administrator role for the change. Avoid using Global Administrator unless the task requires it.
Identify the exact users, groups, roles, apps, or policies affected before making changes.
For Conditional Access, create or modify the policy in Report-only mode first and exclude approved emergency access accounts.
Review Entra sign-in logs or audit logs for pilot impact.
Turn the policy On or save the production setting only after pilot validation.
entra.notes: Conditional: applicable only when the finding involves Entra identities, hybrid privileged accounts, leaked credentials, or cloud roles.

gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings
gpo.setting_name: Ensure custom banned passwords lists are used

steps: Sign in to the Microsoft Entra admin center.
Go to Protection > Authentication methods > Password protection.
Enable custom banned passwords.
Enter organisation-specific banned password terms, one term per line, such as company names, product names, or local slang that users might choose.
Save the settings.
For hybrid AD, ensure on-premises password protection is deployed before expecting the banned list to apply on-premises.
entra.notes: Use Microsoft Entra admin center / Conditional Access / Identity Protection.

steps: Open the Microsoft Defender portal at security.microsoft.com.
In the left navigation, open Exposure Management or Secure Score, then open Microsoft Secure Score.
Open Recommended actions and search for this exact title: "Ensure custom banned passwords lists are used".
Click the recommendation row. Read At a glance, User impact, affected users/devices/entities, and the Implementation section before making any change.
If a Manage button is shown, click Manage to jump to the Microsoft configuration page for this recommendation.

If this is a Device recommendation, open the linked Microsoft Defender Vulnerability Management security recommendation. Device Secure Score actions are remediated from the linked recommendation rather than by manually setting Secure Score status.
Add a note in the recommendation with your change ticket number and planned rollout ring.
After implementation, return to the recommendation and check status. Secure Score points generally take time to refresh; do not mark a device recommendation as complete manually.
defender.notes: Use this for every recommendation to locate Microsoft's tenant-specific implementation text, affected objects, points, and validation status.

validation_steps: Confirm the Secure Score recommendation no longer shows exposed users/devices/entities or that Microsoft shows the action as completed/points awarded.
For device recommendations, verify affected device count in Defender Vulnerability Management after telemetry refresh.

rollback.intune: Remove the configuration policy or set to Not Configured in Intune
rollback.gpo: Set the policy to Not Configured in Group Policy Management
rollback.entra: Set the policy back to Report-only or Off, or revert the setting to its previous value, only through approved change control.

gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings
gpo.setting_name: Change password for accounts with leaked credentials

steps: Sign in to the Microsoft Entra admin center.
Go to Protection > Identity Protection > Risky users.
Filter for users with leaked credentials or high user risk.
Select the affected user.
Require a secure password reset or confirm compromise according to your incident process.
After remediation, dismiss/confirm the risk only when the documented remediation is complete.
entra.notes: Use Microsoft Entra admin center / Conditional Access / Identity Protection.

steps: Open the Microsoft Defender portal at security.microsoft.com.
In the left navigation, open Exposure Management or Secure Score, then open Microsoft Secure Score.
Open Recommended actions and search for this exact title: "Change password for accounts with leaked credentials".
Click the recommendation row. Read At a glance, User impact, affected users/devices/entities, and the Implementation section before making any change.
If a Manage button is shown, click Manage to jump to the Microsoft configuration page for this recommendation.

If this is a Device recommendation, open the linked Microsoft Defender Vulnerability Management security recommendation. Device Secure Score actions are remediated from the linked recommendation rather than by manually setting Secure Score status.
Add a note in the recommendation with your change ticket number and planned rollout ring.
After implementation, return to the recommendation and check status. Secure Score points generally take time to refresh; do not mark a device recommendation as complete manually.
defender.notes: Use this for every recommendation to locate Microsoft's tenant-specific implementation text, affected objects, points, and validation status.

validation_steps: Confirm the Secure Score recommendation no longer shows exposed users/devices/entities or that Microsoft shows the action as completed/points awarded.
For device recommendations, verify affected device count in Defender Vulnerability Management after telemetry refresh.

rollback.gpo: Set the policy to Not Configured in Group Policy Management
rollback.entra: Set the policy back to Report-only or Off, or revert the setting to its previous value, only through approved change control.

steps: Open the Secure Score recommendation "Remove stale Active Directory accounts" and check whether Microsoft lists a Group Policy path or Windows security baseline mapping.
If Microsoft lists a GPO path, open Group Policy Management Console and create a new pilot GPO instead of editing a broad production GPO immediately.
Configure only the exact setting and value Microsoft lists. Do not choose a similar-looking ADMX setting unless it is explicitly named.
Link the GPO to a pilot OU or apply security filtering to a pilot group.
Run gpupdate /force on one pilot device and confirm the effective setting with gpresult.
Expand the GPO only after the pilot device and Secure Score/Defender telemetry validate successfully.
gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings
gpo.setting_name: Remove stale Active Directory accounts

steps: Open the Secure Score recommendation "Remove stale Active Directory accounts" and confirm the recommendation actually has an Entra identity, role, Conditional Access, consent, or authentication component.
Use a least-privileged administrator role for the change. Avoid using Global Administrator unless the task requires it.
Identify the exact users, groups, roles, apps, or policies affected before making changes.
For Conditional Access, create or modify the policy in Report-only mode first and exclude approved emergency access accounts.
Review Entra sign-in logs or audit logs for pilot impact.
Turn the policy On or save the production setting only after pilot validation.
entra.notes: Conditional: applicable only when the finding involves Entra identities, hybrid privileged accounts, leaked credentials, or cloud roles.

defender.portal_path: Microsoft Defender portal > Secure Score or Defender for Identity security posture assessments
steps: Open Microsoft Defender portal > Secure Score or Defender for Identity security posture assessments.
Search/open the exact assessment: Remove stale Active Directory accounts.
Open the affected entities list. Export or copy affected users, groups, computers, GPOs, certificate templates, servers, or domain controllers.
Read the Microsoft remediation text for that assessment before changing AD/AD CS. These recommendations are entity-specific.
Make the AD/Entra/identity change only to the affected entities named by the assessment.
For group membership findings, remove unnecessary privileged membership after owner approval.
For account attribute findings, correct the exact insecure attribute shown by Defender.
For stale/dormant accounts, disable/remove only after owner validation.
defender.notes: Use Defender for Identity assessment output. These are usually AD/AD CS/entity remediation tasks, not generic Intune policies.

steps: Before creating a new policy, check whether Defender already created an Intune security task for this exact recommendation.
In the Intune admin center, go to Endpoint security > Security tasks.
Open the task that matches "Enable 'Microsoft Defender for Endpoint Plug-in for WSL'" if it exists, select Accept, and use the Defender-provided remediation text as the source of truth.
In the Intune admin center, go to Devices > Manage devices > Configuration > Create > New policy.
Select Platform = Windows 10 and later unless the recommendation is explicitly for another OS. Select Profile type = Settings catalog. Click Create.
On Basics, name the policy "Secure Score - 231 - Enable 'Microsoft Defender for Endpoint Plug-in for WSL'". Click Next.
On Configuration settings, click Add settings. In Search, search for: Enable Microsoft Defender for Endpoint Plug-in for WSL.

Add the matching Microsoft setting and configure it to: Enabled / enforced as stated in the recommendation. Do not pick a look-alike setting unless the Secure Score Implementation section confirms it.
Click Next. Assign first to a pilot device group. Do not assign to All devices for the first test unless your change process allows it.
Click Next through Scope tags and Applicability rules if not used. On Review + create, click Create.
Monitor deployment under Devices > Configuration > the policy > Device and user check-in status and Per-setting status.
Open the Secure Score recommendation and check whether Microsoft lists a GPO path or Windows security baseline setting.
If Microsoft lists a GPO path, create/edit a pilot GPO and configure only that exact setting/value.
If no GPO path is listed, do not guess. Use Intune Settings Catalog, Defender security task, or the product-specific method instead.

steps: Open https://intune.microsoft.com.
Go to Endpoint security > Firewall.
Select Create Policy.
Platform: Windows 10 and later.
Profile: Microsoft Defender Firewall.
Name the policy: Secure Score - Disable Microsoft Defender Firewall notifications when programs are blocked for Private profile.
Open Configuration settings.
Configure these settings: Private profile Notifications = False / Display notification = No.
Select Next until Assignments.
Assign to a pilot device group first, then Review + create.
After pilot success, assign to production device groups.
intune.value: Disable blocked-program notifications for Private profile

steps: Open Group Policy Management Console: gpmc.msc.
Create a new pilot GPO or edit your approved security baseline GPO.
Go to: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security > Windows Defender Firewall Properties.
Open the policy setting: Private Profile tab > Settings > Customize.
Set it to: Display a notification = No.
Click OK/Apply.
Link the GPO only to a pilot OU first.
On a pilot device, run gpupdate /force or wait for normal policy refresh.
Reboot the pilot device if the setting requires a restart.
After testing, link the GPO to the wider production OU.

steps: Before creating a new policy, check whether Defender already created an Intune security task for this exact recommendation.
In the Intune admin center, go to Endpoint security > Security tasks.
Open the task that matches "Disable 'Domain member: Disable machine account password changes'" if it exists, select Accept, and use the Defender-provided remediation text as the source of truth.
In the Intune admin center, go to Devices > Manage devices > Configuration > Create > New policy.
Select Platform = Windows 10 and later unless the recommendation is explicitly for another OS. Select Profile type = Settings catalog. Click Create.
On Basics, name the policy "Secure Score - 256 - Disable 'Domain member: Disable machine account password changes'". Click Next.
On Configuration settings, click Add settings. In Search, search for: Disable Domain member: Disable machine account password changes.
Add the matching Microsoft setting and configure it to: Disabled as stated in the recommendation. Do not pick a look-alike setting unless the Secure Score Implementation section confirms it.
Click Next. Assign first to a pilot device group. Do not assign to All devices for the first test unless your change process allows it.
Click Next through Scope tags and Applicability rules if not used. On Review + create, click Create.
Monitor deployment under Devices > Configuration > the policy > Device and user check-in status and Per-setting status.
Open Group Policy Management Console (gpmc.msc).
Create a new GPO for this recommendation or edit your approved security baseline GPO.
Go to: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options.
Open the setting: Domain member: Disable machine account password changes.
Set it to: Disabled.
Link the GPO to a pilot OU or apply security filtering to a pilot group first.
On a pilot device, run gpupdate /force or wait for normal Group Policy refresh.

steps: Open https://intune.microsoft.com.
Go to Endpoint security > Firewall.
Select Create Policy.
Platform: Windows 10 and later.
Profile: Microsoft Defender Firewall.
Name the policy: Secure Score - Disable Microsoft Defender Firewall notifications when programs are blocked for Public profile.
Open Configuration settings.
Configure these settings: Public profile Notifications = False / Display notification = No.
Select Next until Assignments.
Assign to a pilot device group first, then Review + create.
After pilot success, assign to production device groups.
intune.value: Disable blocked-program notifications for Public profile

steps: Open Group Policy Management Console: gpmc.msc.
Create a new pilot GPO or edit your approved security baseline GPO.
Go to: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security > Windows Defender Firewall Properties.
Open the policy setting: Public Profile tab > Settings > Customize.
Set it to: Display a notification = No.
Click OK/Apply.
Link the GPO only to a pilot OU first.
On a pilot device, run gpupdate /force or wait for normal policy refresh.
Reboot the pilot device if the setting requires a restart.
After testing, link the GPO to the wider production OU.
gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security > Windows Defender Firewall Properties
gpo.setting_name: Public Profile tab > Settings > Customize

steps: Open the Microsoft Defender portal at security.microsoft.com.
In the left navigation, open Exposure Management or Secure Score, then open Microsoft Secure Score.
Open Recommended actions and search for this exact title: "Disable Microsoft Defender Firewall notifications when programs are blocked for Public profile".
Click the recommendation row. Read At a glance, User impact, affected users/devices/entities, and the Implementation section before making any change.
If a Manage button is shown, click Manage to jump to the Microsoft configuration page for this recommendation.

If this is a Device recommendation, open the linked Microsoft Defender Vulnerability Management security recommendation. Device Secure Score actions are remediated from the linked recommendation rather than by manually setting Secure Score status.
Add a note in the recommendation with your change ticket number and planned rollout ring.
After implementation, return to the recommendation and check status. Secure Score points generally take time to refresh; do not mark a device recommendation as complete manually.
defender.notes: Use this for every recommendation to locate Microsoft's tenant-specific implementation text, affected objects, points, and validation status.

validation_steps: In Intune, check the Firewall policy Device status and Per-setting status.
On a pilot device, run PowerShell: Get-NetFirewallProfile -Profile Public | Select-Object Name,NotifyOnListen
Confirm Secure Score/Defender recommendation improves after refresh.

rollback.intune: Remove assignment or set the changed firewall setting back to the previous value.
rollback.gpo: Unlink the GPO or set the policy back to Not Configured/previous value.; Run gpupdate /force on a pilot device and confirm rollback with gpresult.
rollback.powershell:
# Rollback
Remove assignment or set the changed firewall setting back to the previous value.

risks: Start with the Defender Secure Score recommendation flyout. It contains the tenant-specific affected objects and Microsoft implementation text. Do not apply broad changes to all users/devices until a pilot succeeds.
Verify compatibility with existing policies before applying.

references.title: Secure Score: Disable Microsoft Defender Firewall notifications when programs are blocked for Public profile

gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings
gpo.setting_name: Only invited users should be automatically admitted to Teams meetings

steps: Open the Secure Score recommendation "Only invited users should be automatically admitted to Teams meetings" and confirm the recommendation actually has an Entra identity, role, Conditional Access, consent, or authentication component.
Use a least-privileged administrator role for the change. Avoid using Global Administrator unless the task requires it.
Identify the exact users, groups, roles, apps, or policies affected before making changes.
For Conditional Access, create or modify the policy in Report-only mode first and exclude approved emergency access accounts.
Review Entra sign-in logs or audit logs for pilot impact.
Turn the policy On or save the production setting only after pilot validation.
entra.notes: Conditional: Entra is relevant for admin roles/groups used to scope or administer the policy, but not the primary implementation plane.

steps: Open the Secure Score recommendation "Set a honeytoken account" and check whether Microsoft lists a Group Policy path or Windows security baseline mapping.
If Microsoft lists a GPO path, open Group Policy Management Console and create a new pilot GPO instead of editing a broad production GPO immediately.
Configure only the exact setting and value Microsoft lists. Do not choose a similar-looking ADMX setting unless it is explicitly named.
Link the GPO to a pilot OU or apply security filtering to a pilot group.
Run gpupdate /force on one pilot device and confirm the effective setting with gpresult.
Expand the GPO only after the pilot device and Secure Score/Defender telemetry validate successfully.
gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings
gpo.setting_name: Set a honeytoken account

steps: Open the Secure Score recommendation "Set a honeytoken account" and confirm the recommendation actually has an Entra identity, role, Conditional Access, consent, or authentication component.
Use a least-privileged administrator role for the change. Avoid using Global Administrator unless the task requires it.
Identify the exact users, groups, roles, apps, or policies affected before making changes.
For Conditional Access, create or modify the policy in Report-only mode first and exclude approved emergency access accounts.
Review Entra sign-in logs or audit logs for pilot impact.
Turn the policy On or save the production setting only after pilot validation.
entra.notes: Conditional: applicable only when the finding involves Entra identities, hybrid privileged accounts, leaked credentials, or cloud roles.

defender.portal_path: Microsoft Defender portal > Secure Score or Defender for Identity security posture assessments
steps: Open Microsoft Defender portal > Secure Score or Defender for Identity security posture assessments.
Search/open the exact assessment: Set a honeytoken account.
Open the affected entities list. Export or copy affected users, groups, computers, GPOs, certificate templates, servers, or domain controllers.
Read the Microsoft remediation text for that assessment before changing AD/AD CS. These recommendations are entity-specific.
Create or choose a dedicated honeytoken account that is not used for real work.
In Defender for Identity entity tags, tag that account as Honeytoken.
Monitor for alerts involving the honeytoken account.
defender.notes: Use Defender for Identity assessment output. These are usually AD/AD CS/entity remediation tasks, not generic Intune policies.

validation_steps: gpresult shows the pilot GPO is applied.
The local effective setting matches the target value.
Secure Score/Defender status improves after telemetry refresh.

rollback.gpo: Set the policy to Not Configured in Group Policy Management
rollback.defender: Use your AD/AD CS backup/change process to reverse only the changed object/permission/policy.

gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings
gpo.setting_name: Set a daily message limit

steps: Open the Secure Score recommendation "Set a daily message limit" and confirm the recommendation actually has an Entra identity, role, Conditional Access, consent, or authentication component.
Use a least-privileged administrator role for the change. Avoid using Global Administrator unless the task requires it.
Identify the exact users, groups, roles, apps, or policies affected before making changes.
For Conditional Access, create or modify the policy in Report-only mode first and exclude approved emergency access accounts.
Review Entra sign-in logs or audit logs for pilot impact.
Turn the policy On or save the production setting only after pilot validation.
entra.notes: Conditional: Entra is relevant for admin roles/groups used to scope or administer the policy, but not the primary implementation plane.

steps: Open the Microsoft Defender portal at security.microsoft.com.
In the left navigation, open Exposure Management or Secure Score, then open Microsoft Secure Score.
Open Recommended actions and search for this exact title: "Set a daily message limit".
Click the recommendation row. Read At a glance, User impact, affected users/devices/entities, and the Implementation section before making any change.
If a Manage button is shown, click Manage to jump to the Microsoft configuration page for this recommendation.

If this is a Device recommendation, open the linked Microsoft Defender Vulnerability Management security recommendation. Device Secure Score actions are remediated from the linked recommendation rather than by manually setting Secure Score status.
Add a note in the recommendation with your change ticket number and planned rollout ring.
After implementation, return to the recommendation and check status. Secure Score points generally take time to refresh; do not mark a device recommendation as complete manually.

gpo.setting_name: Ensure 'Self service password reset enabled' is set to 'All'

Configure Authentication methods, Registration, Notifications, and Customization as required by your organisation.
Save the changes.

gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings
gpo.setting_name: Designate more than one global admin

steps: Review current Global Administrator accounts in the Entra admin center under Identity > Roles & admins > Global Administrator.
Identify at least two approved break-glass/admin accounts according to your internal policy.
Assign Global Administrator only to the minimum number of accounts needed for resilience.
Protect those accounts with phishing-resistant MFA and emergency access procedures.
Document the owners and review schedule.

gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings
gpo.setting_name: Use least privileged administrative roles

steps: In the Entra admin center, go to Identity > Roles & admins.
Review each Global Administrator assignment.
For each person or service, map the work they actually perform to Microsoft's least-privileged role list.
Assign the narrower role first.
Remove Global Administrator only after validating the narrower role works for the admin task.
Repeat until Global Administrator is reserved for approved emergency or top-level administrators only.
entra.notes: Use Microsoft Entra admin center / Conditional Access / Identity Protection.
